Project · sourced research

Building a mobile sauna on a second-hand trailer

A practical, budget guide adapted to the Chilean market and Santiago's climate, for mounting a family sauna on a used carro de arrastre. All units metric, all prices in CLP.

📍 Santiago / RM 🚚 Trailer ~$500.000 CLP 📐 1,7 × 3,0 m 👨‍👩‍👧 Family use 🔌 220V available 🛠️ Intermediate DIY
∑ Open the budget calculator 🧰 See the materials

Headline recommendation

Wood stove, not electric. A real electric heater (6–9 kW) eats almost an entire Chilean single-phase supply (max ~40 A / 8.8 kW for the whole house) and forces an SEC electrician plus a likely three-phase upgrade. A Chilean-made wood stove is cheaper, works off-grid and gives a better sauna. And the one rule above all others: respect the trailer's weight budget — a finished sauna easily reaches 600–800 kg before stove, stones, water and people.

01

🚚 The trailer

Buying & prepping a second-hand trailer

A used "carro de arrastre" (utility trailer) is a sound base for a small sauna, but the single most important concept is the WEIGHT BUDGET: a trailer is rated for a maximum gross weight, and your finished sauna plus everything in it must stay under that minus the trailer's own weight. A 1.7 x 3.0 m footprint is realistic and legal for a light trailer in Chile, but a fully built wood sauna of that size easily weighs 600-800 kg, so you must verify the axle/PBT rating before buying. Around 500.000 CLP on Yapo/MercadoLibre/Chileautos realistically buys an older single-axle or "hechizo" (home-made) trailer that will need inspection and likely new bearings, tires, lights, and possibly springs. Brand-new equivalents (Carromet, Metalbert, Metalcar) run 1.28-1.69M CLP +IVA, so the used route saves real money if the chassis is sound. Legally, any carro de arrastre needs its own patente, permiso de circulacion, revision tecnica and SOAP, and anything over 750 kg of load capacity must have independent brakes.

The weight budget: axle rating minus trailer weight = your sauna budget

Treat the trailer's rated capacity as a hard ceiling. The standard DIY example: a 1588 kg single axle IS the trailer's total load capacity; if the empty trailer weighs ~227 kg, your usable payload (sauna structure + stove + benches + people + water) is only ~1360 kg. A modest 1.5×2.4 m sauna can be built under ~900 kg gross, but a 1.8×3.7 m build runs 1150–1360 kg. Add up framing, insulation, double T&G lining, siding, stove and rocks, benches, and 2-3 bathers, then confirm it sits under the axle rating with margin.

[1] How Much Can a Single Axle Trailer Haul | TrailersPlus ↗

A wood sauna on a 1.7x3 m trailer realistically weighs 600-800 kg

A builder on the permies tiny-house forum estimated his wooden sauna structure alone at about 700 kg and openly worried whether the frame and axle could hold it. The forum advice: find the trailer weight-capacity sticker on the tongue, and prefer well-built travel-trailer frames. For your 1.7x3 m footprint, budget the bare structure at roughly 600-800 kg before stove, rocks, benches and people, which means you want a single axle rated clearly above ~1000-1200 kg, not a flimsy 480-750 kg light utility axle.

[2] Transforming old trailer into Finnish sauna (permies forum) ↗

Design deliberately to stay under a target weight; lighter = towable and legal

Seekingsauna's mobile-sauna writeups stress designing to a fixed weight target (their build aimed under ~907 kg) by choosing a small footprint, thin-but-effective insulation, lightweight lining, and a compact stove. The lighter you build, the easier it is to tow with a normal car and the more headroom you keep under the axle rating. For your occasional-move, grid-power scenario this favors a small electric heater (no firewood weight, no chimney) and a 1.7x3 m or even slightly smaller cabin.

[3] Designing a Mobile Sauna Under 2,000 Pounds - Seeking Sauna ↗

Frame, weld and rust inspection before you hand over cash

Check the main rails and tongue for straightness with no twist or sag; scan for rust especially around welds, crossmembers and the coupler. Good welds look smooth, even and consistent. Reject anything with cracked welds, slag, gaps, heavy grinding-over (a sign of hidden repairs), or rust that has eaten into the steel section. On a cheap Chilean 'hechizo' (home-made) trailer this is the make-or-break check, because frame and weld defects are expensive to fix and unsafe under a top-heavy sauna.

[4] Trailer Inspection Checklist: 25 Things to Check Before You Buy - MaxBuilt ↗

Bearings, leaf springs and tires: the cheap parts you will likely replace

Jack up each wheel and spin it by hand: grinding or play means bad wheel bearings (cheap, but service or replace them before any road trip). Inspect leaf springs/shackles/bushings for sag, cracks or rust; sagging springs change ride height and load behavior. Check tires for matching size, tread (min ~1,6 mm) and, critically, the DOT date code: tires older than ~5 years should be replaced regardless of tread, because trailer tires age out and blow. Budget for new bearings, grease, and a pair of fresh tires on almost any 500.000 CLP used trailer.

[5] How to Inspect a Used Trailer Before You Buy! - Ibex Trailers ↗

What ~500.000 CLP buys vs. a new trailer baseline

On Yapo there are used carros de arrastre around 490.000 CLP (e.g. a unit listed in Paine) up into the millions for horse/heavy units. By comparison, NEW single-axle utility trailers from Chilean makers are a useful price/spec anchor: Carromet's 'Steel 15' is a 1 eje, 750 kg, 2000 x 1500 mm deck, aro 13, no brakes, at $1.280.000 CLP net; Metalbert's 'Romano' 1700 kg single axle with USA aro-13 radials is $1.690.000 +IVA. So 500.000 CLP used is roughly a third of new and worth it only if the chassis passes inspection and the axle rating is adequate.

[6] Carro de arrastre Steel 15 para 750 kg - Carromet ↗

Chilean rules: patente, permiso de circulacion, revision tecnica, SOAP

Every carro de arrastre in Chile, including 'hechizo' ones, needs its own patente, must take out an annual permiso de circulacion at the municipality where it is registered (roughly 15.000-30.000 CLP for a light trailer), pass revision tecnica, and carry SOAP. SOAP for a carro de arrastre is about 3.900 CLP. Revision tecnica is done at a type-B plant on the same calendar as cars (by last plate digit); for capacity under 1750 kg it has three stages: documents, plate check, and braking system. Required docs include SOAP vigente, certificado de homologacion, and the padron. A class B license can tow a trailer up to 3.500 kg of authorized weight.

[7] Como es la Revision Tecnica para carros de arrastre en Chile - PracticaTest ↗

Registration and the 750 kg brake rule (REMUCAR / municipal)

Municipalities register carros de arrastre (e.g. Las Condes via REMUCAR). For a home-made trailer you typically present an invoice or a notarized declaration, your cedula, and the trailer's data: marca/modelo (or 'hechizo'), color, and capacidad de carga. The key threshold: a trailer with load capacity over 750 kg must have an independent braking system. Heavier remolques with PBV at or above 3.860 kg must additionally be inscribed in the Registro Civil's special remolques registry, which a small sauna trailer will be well under.

[8] Registro Municipal de Carros de Arrastre - REMUCAR - Las Condes ↗

Deck preparation: replace the floor with treated pine, keep it light

Most cheap used trailers come with a rusted steel floor or rotten wood deck. For the sauna sub-floor, treated/impregnated Pino Radiata is the standard Chilean exterior option, sold widely (Sodimac, Arauco DECK line, barracas like Madefan, Forestal Arbolito). Arauco DECK is autoclave-impregnated Pino Radiata (MCAz, retention 1 kg/m3 per NCh 819 risk classes R1-R3), good for the under-cabin platform. Build the deck as a flat, sealed, well-fastened base bolted to the frame; keep it minimal in thickness to protect your weight budget, and isolate the hot stove zone from combustible decking.

[9] ARAUCO Chile | Deck (Pino Radiata impregnado) ↗
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Used trailers: search 'carro de arrastre' on Yapo (yapo.cl/autos-accesorios-para-autos-partes-y-piezas-remolque-acoplado), MercadoLibre (vehiculos.mercadolibre.cl/otros/carros-arrastre/usados/), Chileautos (filter tipo 'Hechizo'), and Facebook Marketplace. ~490.000 CLP units exist around Santiago/Paine.
  • New-trailer price anchors so you know if a used deal is fair: Carromet Steel 15 (750 kg, 1 eje, aro 13) $1.280.000 CLP net; Metalbert Romano 1700 kg single axle $1.690.000 +IVA. Other makers: Metalcar, Carromet, Dunkay, Todotaller.
  • Legal load-capacity threshold in Chile: over 750 kg capacity REQUIRES independent brakes (freno). Many cheap light trailers are sold 'sin freno', which caps you at 750 kg capacity unless you add a brake system.
  • Costs to budget: SOAP carro de arrastre ~3.900 CLP/yr; permiso de circulacion ~15.000-30.000 CLP/yr; revision tecnica type-B plant (3 stages for <1750 kg), typically ~5.000-10.000 CLP.
  • Homologacion: revision tecnica asks for a Certificado de Homologacion. A purely 'hechizo' trailer can be trickier to get fully papered; prefer a used trailer that already has patente and papeles al dia, or budget time for the homologation/inscription path (REMUCAR / Registro Civil).
  • Deck/floor wood: treated Pino Radiata impregnado is the cheap, available exterior choice (Sodimac, Arauco DECK, barracas Madefan, Forestal Arbolito, Hensa, Lifewood). Impregnated pine is for the OUTDOOR sub-floor/platform only, never for interior benches or skin-contact surfaces because of the copper/azole preservatives.
  • A class B Chilean license can legally tow up to 3.500 kg authorized weight, far above a small sauna trailer, so towing licence is not the constraint; the trailer's own axle/PBT rating and the brake threshold are.
✅ Tips
  • Before buying, write down your weight budget: target empty trailer weight + sauna structure (~600-800 kg for 1.7x3 m) + stove/rocks + benches + 2-3 people + water, then require the axle/PBT rating to exceed that total with a safety margin.
  • Find the capacity sticker/plate on the tongue or frame; if there is none (common on hechizo trailers), measure the axle diameter and ask the seller, and assume the worst until proven.
  • Take a magnet and a screwdriver to the viewing: the magnet finds bondo over rust, the screwdriver probes suspect frame rust and any wood deck for rot.
  • Jack each wheel and spin/rock it: bearing grumble or wheel wobble = plan to repack or replace bearings (cheap) before towing.
  • Plan to replace tires by DOT date code, not tread; trailer tires older than ~5 years should be changed regardless of looks.
  • Bolt (don't just weld blindly) the sauna base frame to the trailer with through-bolts and load spreaders so weight sits over the axle, slightly forward of center, to keep proper tongue weight.
  • Since 220V grid power is available and the trailer moves rarely, a compact electric heater removes firewood weight, the chimney, and a clearance/fire headache, which is friendlier to both the weight budget and revision tecnica.
  • Keep the build centered over the axle and low; a top-heavy tall sauna on a single axle tows badly and stresses the frame.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Buying on price alone and ignoring the axle/PBT rating, then discovering the finished sauna exceeds the trailer's capacity (unsafe and illegal to tow).
  • Assuming a 750 kg light axle is enough: a wood sauna of this size can hit 600-800 kg bare, leaving no margin for stove, benches, water and people.
  • Overlooking the 750 kg brake rule: a 'sin freno' trailer legally caps load capacity at 750 kg in Chile.
  • Trusting a hechizo trailer with no patente/papeles: getting it homologated, inscribed (REMUCAR) and through revision tecnica afterwards can cost time and may fail.
  • Skipping the weld and frame-rust inspection; cracked or ground-over welds and rotted crossmembers are dangerous under a tall, heavy, top-heavy sauna.
  • Keeping old tires because they 'look fine' tread-wise; aged trailer tires blow out on the road.
  • Using impregnated pine for interior benches or lining; the preservative chemicals are not for hot, skin-contact, high-vapor sauna interiors. Use it only for the outdoor platform/sub-floor.
  • Building tall and off-center, raising the center of gravity and ruining tow stability and tongue weight on a single-axle trailer.
02

🏗️ Structure

Framing & anchoring to the chassis

For a small mobile sauna on a ~1.7 x 3.0 m second-hand trailer, the box is a lightweight stud-frame shell (think tiny-house construction, not a heavy backyard cabin) built as four wall panels plus a sloped roof, bolted down onto a flat subfloor that is itself anchored to the steel chassis. The single biggest constraint is WEIGHT: keep the loaded sauna inside the trailer's rating, mass low and centred slightly forward of the axle, and the heater (the heaviest single item) placed to give correct tongue weight. Use pino radiata for the framing (cheap and everywhere in Chile via Sodimac/Construmart/barracas), pino impregnado or fibrocemento siding for the weather skin, and reserve the few expensive non-resinous woods (álamo, ciprés) for the interior skin and benches only. Anchor with bolts/threaded rods that land on the trailer's cross-members, add diagonal bracing so the box does not rack while towing, and give the roof a real slope (minimum 1:40, ideally a clear shed pitch) with an overhang so rain runs off and away from the walls. In Chile a sub-1.750 kg trailer registers at the municipalidad, so staying light also keeps you legal and simple.

Frame the walls as stud panels, but downsize for weight (2x2/2x3, not 2x4)

Standard sauna walls are a skeleton of vertical studs between a bottom plate and top plate, normally 2x4 at 400 mm on centre. For a MOBILE build you deliberately downsize: a well-known mobile-sauna builder switched from 2x4 to 2x2 studs specifically to save weight and gain interior space ('every centimetre counts'). A sensible compromise for a 1.7x3.0 m box is 2x3 (38x63 mm) framing at ~400 mm on centre, which is light, strong enough for this small span, and a stocked size at Sodimac/barracas. Build each wall flat on the deck, check it is square by measuring both diagonals (the 'X' must be equal), then tilt it up.

[10] A mobile sauna build project that the most amateur builder can finish on their own — Sauna Times ↗

Add blocking where benches and the heater attach

Before you close up the walls, install horizontal blocking (short pieces of the same stud material) between the studs in two places: where the bench brackets will land and where/behind the heater will hang or sit. This gives you solid wood to screw into later instead of relying on thin cladding, and it matters more on a mobile unit where road vibration works fasteners loose. Mark these heights before cladding so you do not have to guess later.

[11] How to Build a Portable DIY Sauna — Family Handyman ↗

Anchor the structure with bolts/threaded rod that land on the trailer cross-members

The structural rule from tiny-house practice: tie the bottom plates to the MAIN frame and cross-members of the trailer, not to thin secondary steel. Best practice is threaded rod (≈16 mm) welded to the chassis, passing up through the floor framing and bottom plate, secured with nut + thick washer (a hold-down plate or fender washer) so it cannot pull through wood. If you cannot weld, drill through the subfloor into the steel and use lag/through bolts plus self-tapping screws where the subfloor meets the frame. Align every anchor with a cross-member so load goes through the frame's strongest runs.

[12] Anchoring A Tiny House To The Trailer — The Tiny Life ↗

Bolt spacing and corner placement

Place the first bolts about 30 cm (a foot) in from each corner, where the steel has a long continuous strong run, then space the rest roughly every 60 cm (two feet) along the bottom plate up to within 30 cm of the far corner. Use nyloc nuts (vibration-proof) or threadlock, washers, and run a bead of construction adhesive (e.g. a polyurethane like Sika) under the bottom plate before bolting so the plate is both glued and bolted. Tie the studs to the bottom plate with metal straps (hurricane/'fleje' ties) and do the same at the top plate-to-rafter joints so the whole box is mechanically linked from roof to chassis to resist road and wind forces.

[13] How to Bolt and Attach a Tiny House to a Trailer — Tiny House Talk ↗

Keep weight low and centred slightly forward of the axle; mind tongue weight

Towing stability depends on the centre of gravity sitting slightly FORWARD of the axle (too far back and the trailer sways/fishtails at speed) and on tongue weight being about 10-15% of total trailer weight. Build heavy items low: floor, water, and especially the heater. On mobile saunas the stove placement is explicitly used to tune tongue weight and handling. Keep the tallest mass (chimney, upper benches) as light and central as possible. A purpose-built mobile sauna can reach ~1.350 kg (~1,35 t), so on a small Chilean trailer you must budget weight aggressively to stay under rating.

[14] Mastering Weight Distribution for Trailer Stability — AAA Trailer ↗

Give the roof a real slope and an overhang for rain runoff

Do not build a flat top. Minimum slope for water runoff is a 1:40 pitch; flat/very-low slopes pond water and leak. A simple shed (mono-pitch) roof is easiest on a rectangular box and sheds rain to one side, away from the door. Add a 30-40 cm overhang to throw water clear of the walls and protect the cladding. For a low-slope roof, an EPDM rubber membrane is the recommended waterproofing; a steeper shed roof can take galvanized/zinc-alum metal sheet (cheap and common in Chile) over a felt/membrane underlay with proper overlaps.

[15] Crafting the Ultimate DIY Outdoor Sauna for Your Garden — Summerhouse24 ↗

Exterior skin: pino impregnado tinglado or fibrocemento siding (both Chile-stocked)

For the weather skin, two budget, locally-available routes: (1) Tinglado (shiplap) de pino impregnado, e.g. 1x5x3.2 m, treated with micronized copper to resist rain and insects — a classic Chilean exterior board sold by barracas and Sodimac. (2) Siding de fibrocemento or PVC from Sodimac, which is lighter-maintenance and rot-proof, a good choice for a unit that sits outdoors and gets towed. Whichever you pick, seal/stain the impregnated pine (it is treated, not finished) and lap boards so water sheds downward; back the cladding with a breathable wrap so wind-driven rain cannot reach the studs.

[16] Tinglado Pino Impregnado 1X5X3,2 — Dimasa ↗

Frame material: pino radiata dimensionado, seco for the structure

The cheap, universal Chilean framing wood is pino radiata 'dimensionado', sold green or kiln-dried (seco) at Sodimac, Construmart and any barraca in 2.4 m and 3.2 m lengths. For a permanent wall/tabique structure choose madera SECA (dry) for dimensional stability — green wood twists as it dries and will rack a small box. Common stocked sections are 1x2 up to 2x4; 2x2 and 2x3 are ideal for this lightweight build. Buy a few extra lengths for the bottom plates, top plates, blocking and roof rafters.

[17] Madera Dimensionada - Pino — Sodimac Chile ↗

Use OSB/structural panel for the subfloor and sheathing, screwed to the frame

A plywood/OSB subfloor ties the floor framing together and gives a flat deck to bolt the walls onto. OSB Estructural 11 mm (122x244 cm sheets) is the standard cheap structural panel at Sodimac. Fasten the subfloor down with self-tapping screws where it meets the steel chassis, and lay it over a vapour barrier. You can also use OSB or terciado as wall sheathing to brace the box against racking (it adds significant stiffness for towing) before applying the breathable wrap and final cladding.

[18] OSB Estructural 11 mm 122x244 cm — Sodimac Chile ↗
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Framing: pino radiata 'dimensionado' is the default cheap stud wood at Sodimac, Construmart and any barraca, in 2.4 m and 3.2 m lengths; choose madera SECA (kiln-dried) for a permanent tabique so it does not twist (https://www.sodimac.cl/sodimac-cl/lista/CATG10763/Madera-dimensionada).
  • Exterior skin options sold here: tinglado de pino impregnado 1x5x3.2 m (barracas / Dimasa, ~$4.061 CLP per board), or fibrocemento / PVC siding at Sodimac and specialists like ChileSiding (https://chilesiding.cl/).
  • Structural panels: OSB Estructural 11 mm 122x244 cm at Sodimac; MercadoLibre Chile lists OSB 11.1 mm from ~$5.500 CLP/plancha (https://listado.mercadolibre.cl/osb-estructural-11,1-mm-valor-$-5.500).
  • Interior skin / benches must be a NON-resinous wood — in Chile use álamo (poplar) or ciprés; avoid pino on bench tops and where skin touches because radiata pine bleeds hot resin and can scald (https://www.construex.cl/exhibidores/tinas_de_madera/producto/madera_sauna_chile).
  • Legal/weight: a carro de arrastre under 1.750 kg cargo capacity registers at the municipalidad (not Registro Civil); max height 4.30 m and combined length 11 m, and it needs SOAP insurance to circulate (https://www.hdi.cl/blog-auto-modo-seguro/3-requisitos-que-deben-cumplir-los-carros-de-arrastre-en-chile/).
  • A genuinely portable carro de arrastre is widely sold here as ~500-750 kg cargo capacity (Lara&Go, Chiletrailers, Campbox) — your second-hand ~500.000 CLP unit is likely in this class, so weight budgeting is the whole game.
  • Hardware: galvanized angle brackets, fleje/hurricane straps, tirafondos (lag bolts), self-tapping pernos and nyloc tuercas are all stocked at Sodimac/Easy/Construmart; use a polyurethane sealant (Sika) under the bottom plate.
✅ Tips
  • Weigh-as-you-go: keep a running tally of every material's weight and compare against the trailer rating BEFORE buying, not after building. The heater + benches + water are your big-ticket masses.
  • Build all four walls flat on the deck and tilt them up; check square by equal diagonals before fixing — a square box is far easier to clad and seal.
  • Place the heater to set tongue weight (~10-15% of total) and keep the centre of gravity just forward of the axle to avoid fishtailing on the road.
  • Sheath the walls with OSB/terciado for racking stiffness — a towed box flexes far more than a static cabin, and diagonal bracing or sheathing stops the corners from loosening.
  • Bolt anchors only into the trailer's main rails and cross-members; mark their positions on the deck before you lay the subfloor so you can drill in the right spots.
  • Glue AND bolt the bottom plate (polyurethane bead + nyloc nuts) so road vibration cannot back fasteners out.
  • Give the roof a clear shed slope plus 30-40 cm overhang; run the low edge to the side away from the door so you do not walk into a curtain of runoff.
  • Reserve expensive non-resinous wood (álamo/ciprés) only for the interior skin and benches — clad the structure and exterior with cheap pino to protect the budget.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Building the box too heavy and blowing past the trailer's rating — easy to do with a thick backyard-cabin frame; downsize studs and panels for a mobile unit.
  • Using green (wet) pino for the structure: it twists as it dries and racks a small towed box. Use madera seca for the frame.
  • Anchoring into thin or secondary steel instead of the chassis cross-members — the bond fails under road shock.
  • A flat or near-flat roof that ponds water and leaks; always exceed the 1:40 minimum slope and add an overhang.
  • Putting resinous radiata pine on bench tops or where bare skin touches — it bleeds hot resin and overheats; that wood belongs on the structure/exterior, not the sauna interior surfaces.
  • Forgetting bench/heater blocking before cladding, then having nothing solid to anchor the heaviest, most safety-critical items into.
  • Loading mass too far back, causing trailer sway; keep the centre of gravity slightly forward of the axle and tongue weight in the 10-15% band.
  • Ignoring Chilean registration/SOAP rules — staying under 1.750 kg keeps it a simple municipal inscription, but it still must be inscribed and insured to be on the road.
03

🧱 Insulation

Insulation & the all-important vapor barrier

For a small sauna on a trailer in central-southern Chile, the single most important detail is getting the wall and ceiling "sandwich" in the right order: framing, then mineral or glass wool in the cavities, then a continuous aluminium-foil vapor barrier on the hot (interior) side, then thin furring strips for a 1.5-2 cm air gap, then the tongue-and-groove cladding. The foil does two jobs at once: it stops humid 90-100 C air from migrating into the wall (where it would condense, rot the frame and grow mold) and it reflects radiant heat back inside so the sauna heats faster and cheaper. Almost every failed DIY sauna gets one of three things wrong: uses cheap polyethylene/plumavit (plastic) instead of foil (it off-gasses and traps moisture at sauna temperatures), forgets the air gap behind the cladding, or leaves foil seams and penetrations unsealed. Aim for roughly R-19 to R-21 walls and R-26 to R-30 (or more) on the ceiling, since heat rises and the ceiling is your biggest loss point. On a trailer you also have to insulate the FLOOR from below and plan for water drainage, which a house-slab sauna does not.

Correct wall/ceiling assembly order (outside to inside)

From exterior to interior: structural frame (studs) with insulation packed in the cavities, then a continuous aluminium-foil vapor barrier stapled to the warm/interior face of the studs, then furring strips (listones) creating a 1.5-2 cm air gap, then the tongue-and-groove interior cladding. The air gap is non-negotiable: it lets radiant heat reflect off the foil and gives any condensation a path to drain instead of sitting against the wood. On the ceiling use the same order but pack it deeper. Install the ceiling foil FIRST, then overlap the wall foil downward over it like roof shingles so moisture runs down and out.

[19] How to Insulate a Sauna: Vapor Barrier, R-Values, and Common Mistakes - Haven of Heat ↗

Why the aluminium-foil vapor barrier is critical (and why plastic fails)

Aluminium foil does double duty: it is a true vapor barrier that keeps moist sauna air out of the wall cavity, AND it reflects radiant heat back into the room so the sauna heats faster and holds temperature. Standard polyethylene plastic sheeting (and EPS/plumavit foam) is unacceptable in a sauna because it softens and can off-gas at sauna temperatures and gives zero radiant reflection. Put the foil on the HOT (interior) side of the insulation only; never sandwich insulation between two vapor barriers or you trap moisture and guarantee rot. Use unfaced (libre) batts when the foil already provides the barrier so you do not create a double barrier.

[20] Sauna Insulation - SaunaTimes ↗

How to seal foil seams and penetrations

Overlap adjoining foil runs by 5–8 cm and seal every seam with genuine aluminium foil tape, pressed down hard with a roller or your fingers. Do NOT use ordinary cloth duct tape or plastic tape, it delaminates and peels in the heat. Every single penetration through the foil (light fixture, heater cable, thermostat, vent, speaker hole) must be wrapped and taped around its full perimeter, because each unsealed hole is a moisture channel straight into the cavity. The goal is one continuous, taped, airtight foil envelope around the whole hot room.

[21] Sauna Vapour Barrier & Insulation Guide - Finnish Sauna Builders ↗

R-value targets: ceiling must beat the walls by ~50%

Because heat rises, the ceiling is the biggest loss point and should be insulated at least 50% heavier than the walls. Practical targets for a cold-ish climate: walls R-15 to R-21 (use a full 5.5 in / ~140 mm of mineral or glass wool), ceiling R-26 to R-30 or more. Never compress the wool to fit, compression reduces R-value proportionally, and a squashed batt insulates far worse than its rating. For a small trailer sauna in central-southern Chile, framing the walls deep enough to hold 100-120 mm wool and the ceiling deep enough for ~150 mm is a sensible, buildable target.

[22] The Ultimate Guide to Sauna Insulation - Haven of Heat ↗

Which insulation materials are SAFE at sauna temperatures

Use mineral wool (lana mineral / Rockwool, the preferred choice, naturally fire-resistant and moisture-tolerant) or unfaced glass wool (lana de vidrio). Both tolerate sauna heat and off-gas minimally. AVOID expanded polystyrene (EPS / plumavit / aislapol) and XPS foam: EPS is only rated for continuous use to about 80 C and short-term to ~100 C, which is exactly sauna range, so it can soften and degrade. Avoid spray foam (inconsistent field curing, off-gassing) and any foil-faced bubble wrap sold as a one-step solution, it is not a substitute for proper wool plus foil.

[23] Poliestireno expandido EPS: propiedades y usos - Aispor (temperature limits) ↗

Moisture, rot and mold prevention beyond the foil

The sealed foil envelope is your primary defense, but it only works if paired with the air gap behind the cladding (so condensation drains) and with ventilation: a low intake near the heater and a high exhaust on the opposite wall let the room dry out after use. Practice the 'bake and breathe' habit, run the heater a bit longer and crack the vents/door after a session to dry the wood and cavity. Trapped moisture from faced batts, missing air gap, or unsealed penetrations is what causes rot and black mold in failed DIY saunas.

[20] Sauna Insulation (bake and breathe, ventilation) - SaunaTimes ↗

Floor treatment and drainage (extra important on a trailer)

Slope the floor about 6 mm per 30 cm (a 1:50–1:30 fall) toward a stainless-steel drain so water runs off and the surface stays non-slip. Finish with a non-porous, heat-and-moisture-tolerant surface: sealed concrete board skim-coated waterproof, ceramic tile over a waterproofing membrane, or a sloped sealed surface topped with removable duckboard grates (rejillas) for foot comfort. Never use carpet or unsealed hardwood. On a trailer, also insulate the floor joists from below (mineral wool) with the vapor barrier on the warm/interior side, and protect the underside from road spray and rodents.

[24] Floor Water-Proofing Tips for DIY Sauna Builders - SaunaVsSteamRoom ↗

What it looks like

🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Glass wool (lana de vidrio) is the cheapest, most available option at Sodimac. Examples: TOPEX lana de vidrio 50 mm R139 0,6x10 m around $9.500 CLP; AISLANGLASS R94 40 mm 1,2x24 m around $41.790 CLP; AISLANGLASS 60 mm 1,2x12 m around $27.544 CLP. Buy 'libre' (unfaced) rolls so the foil is your only vapor barrier. Source: https://www.sodimac.cl/sodimac-cl/lista/CATG10947/Lana-de-Vidrio
  • Lana mineral (Rockwool-type) is the better, more fire-resistant choice and is sold at Sodimac under 'Lana Mineral' (e.g. AISLAN lana mineral 50 mm R122 around $38.700-$39.900 CLP). Costs a bit more than glass wool but tolerates heat better. Source: https://www.sodimac.cl/sodimac-cl/lista/CATG10948/Lana-Mineral
  • Aluminium foil tape (Foil Tape / cinta de aluminio) for sealing seams is at Sodimac: e.g. Foil Tape 50,8 mm x 45 m, and a cinta de aluminio adhesiva 50 mm x 50 m. Buy genuine aluminium tape, NOT cloth duct tape. Source: https://www.sodimac.cl/sodimac-cl/articulo/110326959/Cinta-de-Aluminio-Foil-Tape-50.8-mm-x-45-mts/110326965
  • For the foil vapor barrier itself, look for a foil/FSK barrier (lámina con cara de aluminio) rather than a hidrofuga/Tyvek-style breathable membrane. Sodimac stocks 'barrera de vapor con foil de aluminio'; specialist suppliers like Aislacel sell FSK aluminium radiant+vapor barrier rolls. A breathable hidrofuga membrane goes on the COLD exterior side only, never as the interior sauna barrier. Source: https://www.sodimac.cl/sodimac-cl/lista/CATG10954/Membrana-Hidrofuga-y-Tyvek
  • Wood for cladding/benches in Chile: alamo (poplar) is the local sweet spot, it does not bleed resin, does not splinter, stays cool to the touch and is widely used for tinas and saunas (Hidrotinas, Madefan and barracas sell alamo). Tilo (linden) is also good and gentle on sensitive skin. AVOID radiata pine for bench tops and skin-contact areas, it bleeds resin/alquitran when hot and is prone to mold; reserve cheap pino impregnado for the exterior structure only, never the hot-room interior skin. Sources: https://madefan.cl/ and https://www.hidrotinaschile.cl/
  • Plumavit / aislapol (EPS poliestireno) is everywhere and cheap in Chile, but do NOT use it inside the sauna envelope, its safe continuous temperature (~80 C) is below sauna operating temps. Source: https://aispor.es/blog/propiedades-del-poliestireno-expandido/
✅ Tips
  • Order the layers from inside out as a mantra: cladding, air gap (listones), foil, wool, frame. If you can recite it you will not mess up the build.
  • Buy 'libre' (unfaced) wool so you have exactly ONE vapor barrier (the foil) and never a double barrier that traps moisture.
  • Run the foil up the wall and onto the ceiling continuously, sealing the wall-to-ceiling and wall-to-floor corners with foil tape, these corners leak first.
  • Leave the foil slightly loose, not drum-tight, so it does not tear when the wood frame moves on the road; on a trailer the structure flexes more than a fixed building.
  • Use thin furring strips (listones ~20 mm) over the foil to create the air gap before nailing the alamo cladding.
  • Slope the floor to a stainless drain and lay removable duckboard (rejilla) on top so you can lift it to dry and clean, key on a trailer where water has nowhere to go.
  • Plan ventilation now: a low intake vent near the heater and a high exhaust on the far wall, both closable, so you can 'bake and breathe' the room dry after each use.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Using polyethylene plastic sheeting or plumavit/EPS as the vapor barrier or insulation, it off-gasses, softens at sauna temperature and reflects no heat. Foil + mineral/glass wool only.
  • Forgetting the air gap and nailing cladding straight onto the foil, this kills radiant reflection and traps condensation against the wood.
  • Leaving foil seams or penetrations (light, heater cable, vent) untaped, every gap is a moisture highway into the cavity that rots the frame and grows mold.
  • Compressing the wool to make it fit a shallow frame, a squashed batt loses R-value; frame deep enough for the full thickness instead.
  • Under-insulating the ceiling, it is the biggest heat-loss surface and should be ~50% better insulated than the walls.
  • Using radiata pine or any resinous/splintery wood for bench tops and skin-contact surfaces, hot resin burns and the wood molds; use alamo or tilo for the interior skin.
  • Ignoring the floor on a trailer build, no insulation underneath and no drainage slope leads to a cold, wet, rotting floor that water cannot escape.
  • Putting a breathable hidrofuga/Tyvek membrane on the inside, that belongs on the cold exterior side; the interior sauna side needs the non-breathable foil.
04

🔥 Heater

Wood stove vs electric — the decision

For a trailer sauna of roughly 1.7 x 3.0 m with a usable hot-room volume around 7-9 m3, the heater needs to deliver about 6-9 kW (rule of thumb: ~1 kW per m3, sized up for the cold-ish central-southern winters and any glass). That single number is what makes this decision easy in Chile: a 6 kW electric heater pulls about 28 A and a 9 kW one about 41 A, but a typical Chilean residential empalme monofásico tops out at 40 A / ~8.8 kW for the WHOLE house. So a real electric sauna heater either eats almost your entire single-phase supply or forces you into a 380V trifásico upgrade plus a licensed SEC electrician. A wood stove (estufa a leña para sauna) sidesteps all of that, is genuinely off-grid, costs less to run, and is sold ready-made in Chile by local makers. For an occasionally-moved, budget, family trailer sauna, the wood stove is the stronger fit, with electric only worth it if you already have spare panel capacity and never plan to move off-grid.

Sizing: budget 6-9 kW for a ~7-9 m3 trailer hot room

The industry rule of thumb is roughly 1 kW of heater power per 1 m3 of sauna volume, then size UP for windows, exterior walls, poor insulation and cold climates. A 1.7 x 3.0 m trailer with a ~2 m ceiling is around 9-10 m3 gross; once you frame an insulated hot room inside it you land near 7-9 m3, so you want a 6-8 kW unit (round up to 9 kW if you keep a glass door or skimp on insulation). This same kW figure applies to both electric and wood stoves, which are rated by the cabin volume they can heat.

[25] What Size Sauna Heater Do I Need? - Nordica Sauna ↗

The electric dealbreaker: a Chilean empalme monofásico maxes at ~40 A / 8.8 kW for the whole house

A standard residential single-phase connection in Chile (empalme monofásico) goes up to 40 A at 220V, which is about 8.8 kW total for everything in the property. A 6 kW sauna heater alone draws ~28 A and a 9 kW one ~41 A on 220V single phase, so a real heater either consumes nearly your entire supply (lights, fridge, water heater fighting for the rest and tripping the main) or exceeds it outright. This is the single biggest reason a wood stove is easier in a Chilean home or off-grid trailer.

[26] Empalme monofásico CGE (distribuidora eléctrica) ↗

Electric heaters sold in Chile: 6 kW and 9 kW, 220V/380V, with real amperage figures

Chilean sauna specialists ProSauna (prosauna.cl) and Piscineria sell Keya-type heaters in 3, 4.5, 6, 9 and 12 kW. ProSauna's own listing states the 9 kW unit needs a dedicated 41 A connection and heats up to 9 m3, and notes it ships as 220V monofásico but can be reconfigured to 380V trifásico. Pricing is not shown online (quote by WhatsApp/phone), but the published specs confirm that anything 6 kW and up needs its own heavy dedicated circuit, and 9 kW realistically wants three-phase.

[27] Calefactor Sauna 9KW 220V/380 - ProSauna ↗

If you go electric: dedicated breaker, RCD and AWG 6-8 cable, installed by a registered electrician (SEC)

A 6 kW heater at ~28 A needs its own circuit: a ~30-32 A automatic breaker plus a diferencial (RCD), wired in roughly AWG 8 (8.4 mm2) to AWG 6 (13.3 mm2) copper per Chilean SEC equivalence tables; a 9 kW unit at ~41 A pushes you to AWG 6 and ideally 380V trifásico. This is not DIY-legal in Chile: by law it must be declared and done by an SEC-authorized installer, and the panel/empalme may need upgrading. Budget for an electrician plus a possible aumento de potencia.

[28] SEC - Apéndice 6: equivalencia de secciones AWG-mm2 ↗

Wood stoves are sold ready-made in Chile and are the cheaper, off-grid path

BuenaCaldera (buenacaldera.cl) sells purpose-built sauna wood stoves (Estufa Leña Sauna 15A-15D) from about $504.000 to $660.000 CLP, plus a chimney kit around $386.000 CLP and volcanic stones at ~$16.000 CLP per 25 kg bag. Nordicasa offers a Finnish-design stove made in Chile (60x60x100 cm, 8-18 m3, stones and cañones included) at $1.290.000 CLP. A wood stove needs no electrical supply at all, which suits an occasionally-moved trailer and patchy off-grid sites.

[29] Estufas Sauna a leña - BuenaCaldera ↗

Wood stove safety: clearances, non-combustible base and a proper flue (critical inside a wood trailer)

A wood sauna stove must sit on a non-combustible base (Nordicasa specifies a 1x1 m fireproof base), keep clearance to combustible walls (manufacturers commonly want generous separation, with heat shields if tight), and vent through a metal flue that clears the roofline and stays away from any combustible material. Inside an all-wood trailer this is the main hazard: use a double-wall insulated chimney through the roof with a proper roof-penetration kit and a spark arrestor, plus a fresh-air inlet for combustion.

[30] Estufa Sauna a Leña - Nordicasa ↗

Trade-offs: heat-up time, maintenance and ambience

Electric heats fast and is set-and-forget (flick a switch, ready in ~20-40 min, easy timer control), with no ash, no flue and no fuel storage. Wood takes about 30-60 min to come up to temperature, needs you to chop/store dry firewood and clean ash, but gives the deep radiant heat, crackle and scent of a traditional Finnish sauna and reaches higher temperatures. For occasional family weekend use, the slower wood ritual is usually a feature, not a chore.

[31] Sauna Wood Stoves vs Electric Heaters - Muskoka Sauna Co ↗

Wood choice near skin matters more than heater type: use álamo for benches, never resinous pino

Whatever heater you pick, benches and any wood you touch should be a low-resin, low-conductivity species. Álamo (poplar) is the realistic Chilean choice: it does not secrete resin, does not splinter and does not overheat, ideal for asientos. Avoid raw pino radiata and spruce on benches and backrests: heated resin melts, sticks to skin, can burn and stains the wood. Radiata pino is fine for the outer structure but keep it off skin-contact surfaces unless thermally modified.

[32] Cómo elegir la madera adecuada para una sauna - Fluidra ↗

What it looks like

Reference costs

ItemPrice (CLP)Note
Estufa a leña para sauna (Chilean-made, BuenaCaldera 15A-15D) [29]$504.000 - $660.000 CLPPurpose-built sauna wood stove; volume rating not published per model, confirm by WhatsApp.
Estufa sauna a leña premium (Nordicasa, 8-18 m3, stones + flue included) [30]$1.290.000 CLPFinnish design made in Chile, 60x60x100 cm, made-to-order ~4 weeks, needs 1x1 m fireproof base.
Kit de chimenea para estufa de sauna (BuenaCaldera) [29]$386.000 - $435.000 CLPEssential safety item; use double-wall insulated flue through the trailer roof.
Piedras volcánicas para sauna (saco 25 kg) [29]$16.000 CLPNeed roughly 20-30 kg depending on the stove's stone box.
Calefactor eléctrico sauna 6 kW (Keya, 220V/380V) ProSauna/Piscineria [33]Quote only (not listed)~28 A; needs dedicated 30 A breaker + RCD + AWG 8 cable + SEC electrician. Price by WhatsApp/phone.
Calefactor eléctrico sauna 9 kW (Keya, up to 9 m3) [27]Quote only (not listed)~41 A; exceeds standard 40 A single-phase empalme, realistically wants 380V trifásico.
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • A standard Chilean single-phase connection (empalme monofásico) maxes out at 40 A / ~8.8 kW for the WHOLE house (source: CGE). A 6 kW heater alone already draws ~28 A, leaving almost nothing for the rest of the house.
  • For 9 kW or heavy use you really want 380V three-phase (trifásico), which means a power-capacity upgrade with your utility (CGE, Enel or Chilquinta depending on the zone) and a licensed SEC installer. Doing this yourself is not legal in Chile.
  • Electric sauna heaters in Chile: ProSauna (prosauna.cl) and Piscineria sell Keya-type units in 3 / 4.5 / 6 / 9 / 12 kW; prices by quote (WhatsApp/phone), not published online.
  • Harvia has a Chilean representative (harvia.cl, Estación Central, Santiago) if you prefer a premium brand, but at a higher cost than the generic Keya units.
  • Chilean-made sauna wood stoves: BuenaCaldera (Estufa Leña Sauna 15A–15D) $504.000–$660.000 CLP; Nordicasa (Finnish design, 8–18 m³) $1.290.000 CLP. BuenaCaldera chimney kit ~$386.000 CLP.
  • Volcanic sauna stones are sold by the 25 kg sack (~$16.000 CLP at BuenaCaldera; also tinascalientes.cl). You need 20–30 kg depending on the heater.
  • Bench wood: álamo (poplar) is the low-cost, resin-free local choice. Radiata pine is abundant and cheap (Sodimac / Easy / Construmart / barracas) but for the structure only, never for skin-contact surfaces.
  • Generic wood stoves (salamandras) also appear on MercadoLibre Chile from ~$57.000 CLP, but a proper sauna stove with a stone box and/or water tank is the right tool; an ordinary salamandra isn't designed to hold stones or reach sauna temperatures.
✅ Tips
  • Recommendation for THIS user: go WOOD. With occasional moves, possible off-grid sites and a tight budget, a Chilean-made sauna wood stove (BuenaCaldera ~$504.000-$660.000 CLP + chimney) avoids the empalme/trifásico headache entirely and gives a better sauna experience for the money.
  • Buy a stove rated for MORE than your m3, not less. A unit rated 8-18 m3 (like Nordicasa's) on a 7-9 m3 cabin heats fast and you simply burn less wood; an undersized one struggles in winter.
  • Spend on the chimney, not just the stove. A double-wall insulated flue with a proper roof-penetration kit and spark arrestor is the real safety item in an all-wood trailer.
  • Mount the stove over a non-combustible base (Nordicasa asks for 1x1 m) and add a rear heat shield with an air gap so you can keep the stove a safe distance from the wood wall without wasting cabin space.
  • Use álamo for benches, backrests and headrests; keep cheap pino radiata for the hidden frame only. Never seal interior wood with varnish or barniz (it off-gasses and blisters in heat).
  • Only choose electric if you already have spare panel capacity AND will always run on grid: then a 6 kW unit on a dedicated 30 A breaker + RCD + AWG 8 cable, installed by an SEC electrician, is the simplest set-and-forget option.
  • Keep a fresh-air inlet low near the stove for combustion air, plus an adjustable high vent for the sauna itself; a sealed trailer will smother the fire and the bathers.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Assuming a 220V plug is enough for an electric sauna heater. A 6 kW heater needs ~28 A on its own dedicated circuit, not a normal wall outlet, and a 9 kW one (~41 A) exceeds a standard 40 A single-phase empalme entirely.
  • Budgeting only for the heater. Electric hides the cost of an SEC electrician, dedicated breaker/RCD, heavy AWG 6-8 cable and possibly an aumento de potencia or 380V upgrade; these can rival or exceed the heater price.
  • Adapting a generic salamandra/estufa a leña común as a sauna stove. Ordinary wood stoves are not built to hold a stone box, reach sauna temperatures, or let you throw water (löyly); buy a purpose-made estufa de sauna.
  • Under-spec'ing or DIY-ing the chimney in a wood trailer. Single-wall pipe too close to wood framing is the classic cause of sauna/cabin fires; use insulated double-wall flue and correct clearances.
  • Putting pino radiata or spruce where skin touches. Heated resin melts onto skin, can burn and permanently stains the bench. Use álamo (poplar) for all contact surfaces.
  • Sealing or varnishing interior sauna wood. Coatings off-gas and blister at sauna temperatures; interior wood must stay bare.
  • Ignoring trailer movement: a heavy stove and stone load (20-30 kg of stones plus the stove) must be bolted down and weight-distributed, or it shifts/damages the structure when you occasionally tow it.
05

⚠️ Fire safety

Clearances, heat shields, flue & CO

For a trailer sauna in central-southern Chile, the heater is the single most dangerous element: a wood stove in a tiny, tight, mobile box runs hot, vibrates on the road, and produces carbon monoxide (CO). The non-negotiables are: respect the manufacturer's clearance-to-combustibles around the stove and single-wall stovepipe, build a non-combustible floor and wall heat shield with a ventilated air gap, use a double-wall (insulated) flue where it passes through the roof with proper flashing and a spark arrestor, feed the fire a dedicated fresh-air intake plus an exhaust vent, and install a battery CO detector. Failing to respect clearances is the leading cause of sauna fires, and CO poisoning is the leading cause of sauna deaths. Electric is genuinely safer on the fire/CO front (no flame, no flue, no CO), so given you usually have 220V it is worth weighing seriously; but if you go wood, the rules below are mandatory, not optional. Everything here is buildable with materials sold at Sodimac, Easy, Construmart and MercadoLibre Chile.

Clearance to combustibles: the core distances

General minimum rear/side clearance from a wood sauna stove body to a bare combustible wall is roughly 400-600 mm; some models with a built-in rear shield drop to ~200 mm. Single-wall stovepipe (the connector inside the room) needs about 460 mm to any combustible. ALWAYS follow the exact figure on the manufacturer's plate, as it overrides any general rule. In a 1.7 m wide trailer you almost certainly cannot meet 460 mm to the wall on a bare wall, which is exactly why a ventilated heat shield (below) is required, not optional.

[34] Sauna Wood Stove Guide: How to Size and Install One ↗

Ventilated heat shield: how much it buys you and the air-gap rule

A wall-mounted air-cooled heat shield with a 1-inch (25 mm) ventilated air gap behind it, open at top and bottom for convection, reduces single-wall stovepipe/connector clearance by up to 66% (460 mm becomes ~150 mm). A shield attached directly to the stove cuts clearance in half. Use 0,6 mm-or-thicker sheet metal OR cement board 12 mm+ (fibrocemento/Volcanboard works), held off the wall on 1-inch ceramic or metal spacers with heat-proof screws. Never bolt the shield flat to the wall: the air gap IS the protection. The shield should extend ~450 mm beyond each edge and ~500 mm above the top of the stove.

[35] Heat Shields to Reduce Clearance Requirements - Tiny Wood Stove ↗

Non-combustible floor protection (hearth)

Build a non-combustible hearth under and around the stove extending at least 300 mm on all sides of the base and ~450-500 mm in front of the loading door to catch embers. On a trailer, a sensible stack is steel sheet over fibrocemento/cement board over the wood deck, optionally topped with stone or ceramic. This both stops radiant heat from charring the deck and contains falling coals when you load wood. Keep it rigid and screwed down so road vibration cannot shift it.

[34] Sauna Wood Stove Guide: How to Size and Install One ↗

Double-wall insulated flue through the roof, with flashing and storm collar

The connector pipe inside the room can be single-wall, but where the flue passes through the roof (and the ceiling) it MUST be double-wall insulated (Class A type) chimney pipe, which keeps the outer surface cool enough to protect the roof. A complete kit includes single-wall connector, an insulated ceiling support/penetration plate, the double-wall section, roof flashing sized to the pipe and roof pitch, a storm collar, and a rain cap. The roof penetration must be fully watertight; get the flashing-to-pitch match right or it will leak. This is the part most worth doing carefully or having checked.

[36] Let's make purchasing wood stove chimney components easy | SaunaTimes ↗

Chimney height, draft and spark arrestor for a mobile sauna

For a trailer sauna the chimney should terminate at least 300–600 mm above the highest point of the roof to get reliable draft; a detachable/extendable top section helps both draft and road clearance. Cap with a rain cap that includes a spark arrestor screen (about 3/4-inch / ~19 mm mesh) to stop hot embers landing on a dry Chilean-summer roof or grass. Note the tradeoff: a tight mesh cap can worsen back-puffing in some installs, so start a small hot fire to warm the flue fast and add a damper to fine-tune draft.

[37] Information on Mobile Sauna Ventilation and Drafting - KUUMA ↗

Dedicated combustion-air intake plus exhaust vent

A wood sauna needs three openings: (1) a low intake vent feeding fresh air to the stove, ideally a 100–150 mm duct 150-300 mm from the stove at floor level, separate from the bathers' air; (2) air at the base of the wall behind/under the stove; and (3) a high exhaust vent on the opposite wall, 150-300 mm below the ceiling, sized to match or exceed the intake. In a tiny tight trailer this is critical: without makeup air the stove starves, combustion goes incomplete, and CO builds up. Make the vents closeable to tune temperature once it is running.

[37] Information on Mobile Sauna Ventilation and Drafting - KUUMA ↗

Carbon monoxide: the real killer, and why NOT to add a fan

CO is colorless and odorless; incomplete combustion in a small tight space produces it and it can accumulate fast, which is why mobile saunas are higher risk. Install a battery-powered CO detector mounted on the wall, NOT on the hot ceiling, and test it before every burn. Critically: do NOT install an exhaust fan in a wood-burning sauna unless you know exactly what you are doing, because a fan creating negative pressure competes with the chimney draft and can pull combustion gases (CO) back into the room instead of up the flue.

[38] Ventilation and Drafting a Sauna Stove - KUUMA ↗

Keep firewood and combustibles away from radiant heat

Radiant heat off a sauna stove can char or ignite anything stored too close, including the wood you are about to burn, towels, and the bench skirt. Never stack firewood against or directly beside the stove; keep it outside the stove's clearance zone (treat it like a combustible wall, ~450 mm minimum) or store it outside the cabin entirely. The same applies to the wooden bench edge nearest the stove: protect it with the same shield/clearance logic, since benches and trim sit right in the radiant field.

[39] Clearances to Combustible Materials for Fireplaces & Stove Pipe ↗

What it looks like

🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Wood sauna stoves are sold locally: BuenaCaldera (buenacaldera.cl) lists Estufa Leña Sauna models 15A/15B/15C/15D from $504.000-$660.000 CLP, plus a sauna chimney kit at ~$386.000 CLP and a 'chimney full set' ~$435.000 CLP; sauna stones (piedra) ~$16.000 CLP per 25 kg sack. Nordicasa (nordicasa.cl) sells a Finnish-design wood sauna stove made in Chile for 8-18 m3 rooms.
  • Buying the matched factory chimney kit (single-wall connector + insulated ceiling/roof penetration + flashing + storm collar + spark-arrestor cap) is the safest path and avoids improvising the roof penetration, which is the highest-leak, highest-fire-risk joint.
  • For the heat shield and hearth, Sodimac/Easy stock Volcanboard fibrocemento: 4 mm ~$7.590, 5 mm ~$10.990, 6 mm ~$12.990, 8 mm ~$19.990 (120x240 cm sheets). Volcanboard is rated non-combustible (Clase A1) and is the cheap, available board for shields, ceiling penetration backing and hearth build-up.
  • Galvanized or stainless steel sheet (plancha de acero/zincalum) for the shield face and hearth top is available at Sodimac, Easy, Construmart and barracas; pair it with the fibrocemento on ceramic spacers.
  • CO detectors are sold at Sodimac (AIZO autonomous CO alarm; VIPA WiFi CO sensor; 2-in-1 smoke+CO units) and on MercadoLibre Chile; budget roughly $10.000-$30.000 CLP for a basic battery autonomous unit. Buy a battery model so it works with the engine off and away from grid.
  • Chile has no single national 'sauna code', but mobile/road use means the trailer build should still respect SEC electrical norms if you also wire 220V, and the chimney height/spark arrestor matter especially given high summer wildfire risk in central-southern Chile (CONAF burn restrictions).
✅ Tips
  • Read the stove's data plate and copy its EXACT clearance numbers before you frame anything; the general 400–600 mm figures are only a fallback.
  • Build the wall shield as: fibrocemento (Volcanboard 6 mm) + steel sheet face, held off the wall on 25 mm ceramic spacers, open top and bottom so air convects behind it. The gap is the protection, not the board.
  • Use single-wall connector pipe inside the room (it sheds heat into the sauna, which you want) and switch to double-wall insulated pipe for the ceiling and roof pass-through only.
  • Terminate the flue 300-600 mm above the highest roof point and fit a rain cap with ~19 mm spark-arrestor mesh; carry a spare extendable top section for road transit.
  • Cut three vents: low intake near/under the stove (100-150 mm), a base-of-wall vent behind the stove, and a high exhaust on the far wall; make them closeable.
  • Mount a battery CO detector at wall height (not the scorching ceiling) and physically test it before each session.
  • Keep a fire extinguisher rated for ordinary combustibles (ABC dry-chemical, sold at Sodimac/Construmart, ~$15.000-$30.000 CLP for a small unit) and a metal ash bucket with a lid right by the door; never empty hot ashes onto dry ground.
  • Store firewood outside the cabin or well outside the stove's radiant clearance zone; treat the woodpile like a combustible wall.
  • Since you usually have 220V, seriously price an electric heater alternative: it removes the flue, roof penetration, spark and CO risks entirely, which is a large safety win for a tiny mobile box.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Bolting a heat shield flat against the wall with no air gap: this traps heat and can be worse than no shield. It needs the ~25 mm ventilated gap.
  • Running single-wall pipe through the roof/ceiling instead of double-wall insulated pipe: single-wall stays hot and can ignite the roof structure.
  • Improvising the roof flashing or using the wrong pitch flashing: leaks rot the roof, and a sloppy penetration is also a fire gap. Match flashing to pipe diameter and roof pitch.
  • Installing an exhaust fan with a wood stove: negative pressure can backdraft CO into the room. Use passive vents instead.
  • No fresh-air intake: in a tight trailer the stove starves, combustion goes incomplete, smoke and CO rise, and you get back-puffing.
  • Skipping the CO detector or putting it on the hot ceiling where it cooks and fails. Mount it on the wall and test it.
  • Stacking firewood, towels or kindling against the stove to dry: radiant heat ignites them. Keep everything outside the clearance zone.
  • Relying on general internet clearance numbers over the manufacturer's plate, or ignoring that road vibration can loosen the flue joints and shift the hearth, both of which must be re-checked after every move.
  • Emptying hot ash onto dry grass in a Chilean summer: serious wildfire risk. Use a lidded metal ash can and let ash go cold.
06

🪵 Woods

Chilean wood species for benches & cladding

Inside a sauna, wood is chosen for safety, not looks: bench and any skin-contact surface must be low-density and low thermal conductivity (so it stays cool and does not burn you at 80-90 C), free of resin (resin melts, sticks, burns and stains), free of knots, and splinter-free. The classic Nordic woods (spruce, aspen, western red cedar, abachi, alder) are either not stocked or only sold as expensive imported/specialty lumber in Chile, so the realistic, buildable answer here is: alamo (poplar) is the single best locally-available bench/skin-contact wood and is genuinely the same genus as aspen; for non-skin cladding (walls/ceiling) you have far more freedom, including native lenga, coigue and even tinglado de pino if you accept some knots and minor resin away from skin. Pino radiata is the cheap default everywhere in Chile (Sodimac/Easy/barracas) and is fine for the hidden structure/framing and arguably for ceiling/upper-wall cladding, but it is the wrong choice for benches and backrests because it is knotty and resinous. Never varnish or lacquer the inside; leave it raw or use only a breathable paraffin/sauna oil.

Bench wood must be low thermal conductivity, resin-free, knot-free — the physics

Sauna benches and any surface skin touches need wood that stays cool and never melts. Ranked by thermal conductivity the classic bench woods are abachi (~0.09 W/mK), aspen (~0.10), western red cedar (~0.11), alder (~0.11-0.12) and Nordic spruce (~0.12). Lower number = stays cooler to the touch and far less likely to burn skin at 80-90 C. The bench wood must also be resin-free (resin melts, sticks to skin, burns and stains), knot-free (knots get hot and bleed sap) and fine-grained so it does not splinter on bare skin.

[40] Best Wood for a Sauna: Cedar, Hemlock, Aspen & More Compared — Peak Primal Wellness ↗

Alamo (poplar) is the best LOCAL bench wood in Chile — it is basically aspen

Alamo belongs to the same family/genus context as aspen (Populus), has a pale, smooth, near-odorless surface and meets every bench requirement: it does not secrete resin, does not splinter, and does not overheat, so it is comfortable for direct skin contact. Spanish-language sauna references explicitly call alamo/alamo temblon (aspen) one of the most desired woods for sauna benches. In Chile alamo is commercially grown and sold dry (alamo seco) on MercadoLibre Chile and at specialty barracas, making it the realistic, affordable substitute for imported aspen. Use it for bench tops, backrests, headrests and any slat you sit or lean on.

[32] Como elegir la madera adecuada para una sauna — Fluidra ↗

AVOID pino and spruce on benches/backrests — resin burns and stains skin

Pine (pino) and Norwegian spruce both secrete resin/sap when heated; the resin melts, sticks to skin, causes burns and leaves stubborn stains. They are acceptable only for exterior or non-skin surfaces (upper walls, ceiling) where nobody leans against them, and even then you should pick low-knot boards. Quality sauna wood for seating must be knot-free and splinter-resistant. Bottom line for Chile: do not put pino radiata where bare skin sits or leans — that is the one hard no.

[32] Como elegir la madera adecuada para una sauna — Fluidra ↗

Pino radiata is fine for the hidden STRUCTURE/framing, not for skin surfaces

For the wall/bench framing and studs behind the foil vapour barrier, plain kiln-dried (seco) pino radiata is the normal, accepted choice — use untreated kiln-dried lumber, never pressure-treated/impregnated wood (the green CCA/chemical-treated stuff), because pressure-treated wood off-gasses toxins under sauna heat. Best practice is to leave an air gap behind the cladding with 1x4 (or local 1x2/2x2) spacer battens between studs and the visible lining, which prevents mould in a well-ventilated sauna. Pino radiata is the cheapest framing timber in Chile and is correct here precisely because it is hidden and never touched.

[41] Building a Sauna: Framing, Insulation, and Vapor Barrier Guide — Peak Primal Wellness ↗

Native lenga, coigue, rauli, mañio for CLADDING (walls/ceiling), not benches

For the visible wall and ceiling lining (no skin pressure), Chilean native hardwoods are a beautiful, durable, locally-stocked option sold as tongue-and-groove revestimiento. Lenga is native (Coyhaique/Aysen), dimensionally stable, light reddish tone, good in humid interiors. Coigue is a hard, durable native, white-brown to reddish, sold as interior cladding. Rauli and mañio are also offered as 20 mm kiln-dried revestimiento. Caveat: these are hardwoods (denser, heavier, hold heat more than alamo), so they are great for walls/ceiling but a poorer choice than alamo for a bench top you sit on for long sessions. They are also pricier than pino.

[42] Revestimiento Coihue — WMaderas ↗

Cheapest cladding: pino tinglado/machihembrado from Sodimac/Easy

If budget rules, the cheapest sauna lining in Chile is pino tinglado (overlap/shiplap) or machihembrado (tongue-and-groove) seco cepillado, stocked at Sodimac and Easy. Pino Tinglado Seco Cepillado 19×125 mm × 3,2 m runs about $3.090 CLP per pieza at Sodimac. This is acceptable for ceiling and upper walls (away from skin); choose the lowest-knot boards you can hand-pick, and reserve alamo for the bench zone. Pino machihembrado 3/4x5 x 3.2 m is also stocked for the same use.

[43] Pino Tinglado Seco Cepillado 19×125 mm × 3,2 m — Sodimac Chile ↗

Thermo-modified (termotratada) radiata pine — the premium bench upgrade made in/near Chile

Thermally modified radiata pine (heated >200 C in an oxygen-free chamber) loses its resin and most of its movement: the result has no knots issue, releases no resin, does not splinter and stays cool — explicitly described as ideal for sauna benches. Because radiata pine is the dominant Chilean plantation species, thermo-treated radiata is produced regionally (Topwood Duolux and similar suppliers sell madera termotratada in Chile). It is more expensive than raw pino but far cheaper than imported cedar/abachi and is the best-value high-end bench/cladding option if you want a darker, fully stable, resin-free wood without importing.

[44] 5 razones por las que la madera termotratada Thermowood es la mejor madera para saunas — Lunawood ↗

Treatment: leave it RAW or use only paraffin/sauna oil — never varnish or lacquer

Inside the hot room you must not seal the wood with varnish, lacquer or ordinary barniz: those films trap moisture, peel, and release harmful fumes when heated. The correct approach is to leave the wood bare, or treat only every 6-12 months with a breathable wood preservative — paraffin-based sauna oil (aceite/parafina para sauna) on benches and headrests. Clean with a soft brush and water, never a pressure washer. Note that lenga/coigue cladding accepts oils well, but inside a sauna you still skip the lacquer.

[32] Como elegir la madera adecuada para una sauna — Fluidra ↗

Native hardwood cladding pricing per m2 (real barraca quotes)

Concrete Chilean per-m2 revestimiento (cladding) prices from a native-wood barraca, 20 mm kiln-dried (seco camara): Coigue ~$41.412/m2, Rauli ~$56.834/m2, Mañio ~$56.834/m2. These are real interior-lining hardwoods and let you budget the difference vs pino tinglado (roughly $3.090 per 3.2 m pieza of 5 cm width, i.e. far cheaper per m2). For a small 1.7 x 3.0 m sauna, doing benches in alamo and walls/ceiling in pino tinglado keeps cost low; an all-native-wood interior is a real but markedly pricier upgrade.

[45] Maderas — Madera Nativa Chilena ↗

What it looks like

Reference costs

ItemPrice (CLP)Note
Pino Tinglado Seco Cepillado 19×125 mm x 3,2 m (cheapest cladding, walls/ceiling away from skin) [43]~$3.090 CLP por piezaSodimac. Pick low-knot boards; not for skin-contact surfaces.
Pino Certificado Seco Cepillado 2x6 x 3,2 m (framing / structure) [46]~$7.990 CLP (Easy) / ~$8.300 IVA inc (Chocalan)Kiln-dried, untreated — use for hidden bench/wall framing only.
Coigue revestimiento (interior cladding), 20 mm seco camara [45]~$41.412 CLP/m2Native hardwood; good for walls/ceiling, heavier/hotter than alamo for benches.
Rauli revestimiento, 20 mm seco camara [45]~$56.834 CLP/m2Native hardwood cladding; premium look, pricier.
Mañio revestimiento, 20 mm seco camara [45]~$56.834 CLP/m2Native softwood/lighter species; cladding use.
Alamo seco (poplar) — best local bench wood [47]Varies; sold by tabla/pieza on MercadoLibre Chile and barracasNot consistently big-box stocked; source via ML 'alamo seco' or specialty barraca. Confirm current per-pieza price with seller.
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Alamo (poplar, genus Populus, ~aspen) is the realistic local stand-in for imported aspen and the best LOCAL bench wood. Sold as 'alamo seco' on MercadoLibre Chile and at specialty barracas; not always stocked at big-box stores, so check barracas and ML classifieds.
  • Western red cedar and abachi (the classic premium sauna woods) are essentially import-only / specialty in Chile and expensive — do not plan a budget build around them.
  • Native cladding hardwoods are genuinely available: lenga (from Coyhaique/Aysen, light reddish, stable), coigue (hard, durable, white-brown to reddish), rauli and mañio. Suppliers: WMaderas, Madera Nativa Chilena, Maderas Tranapuente, Anihue, Forestal Maihue.
  • Native revestimiento pricing (20 mm seco camara): coigue ~$41.412/m2, rauli ~$56.834/m2, mañio ~$56.834/m2 (Madera Nativa Chilena / WMaderas).
  • Cheapest cladding is pino tinglado / machihembrado seco cepillado at Sodimac and Easy: e.g. Pino Tinglado Seco Cepillado 19×125 mm × 3,2 m about $3.090 CLP/pieza at Sodimac.
  • Pino radiata (the dominant Chilean plantation wood) is cheap and everywhere — correct for hidden framing (kiln-dried, NOT impregnated/pressure-treated), wrong for benches and skin-contact surfaces because of knots and resin.
  • Thermo-modified radiata pine (madera termotratada) is produced regionally (e.g. Topwood Duolux) and is the best-value resin-free dark upgrade without importing.
  • Use paraffin/sauna oil only (parafina/aceite para sauna); never barniz or laca inside the hot room.
✅ Tips
  • Build the bench and backrest from alamo seco, hand-picking knot-free, straight-grained boards; use hidden stainless or galvanized fasteners countersunk below the surface so no hot metal touches skin.
  • Round over (chamfer/sand) every bench edge and corner — splinter prevention matters more than species on a surface you sit on bare.
  • Run cladding boards horizontally on walls is fine, but a vertical orientation sheds condensation better; whatever you choose, leave a ventilated air gap behind it with 1x2/2x2 pino battens.
  • Mix to save money: pino tinglado on the ceiling and upper walls (no skin contact), alamo on the bench and the lower 'lean-back' zone where your skin actually touches.
  • If you can stretch the budget, do the bench top in thermo-treated radiata or alamo and the rest in pino — that single upgrade buys the most comfort and safety per peso.
  • Buy the wood kiln-dried (seco/seco camara), let it acclimate inside the trailer cabin for a few days before fixing, and leave small expansion gaps between boards.
  • Use stainless or hot-dip galvanized screws/nails everywhere inside — plain steel rusts and bleeds black stains in the humid heat.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Putting pino or spruce where skin sits/leans: resin melts at sauna temperature, sticks to and burns skin, and leaves permanent stains. This is the classic beginner mistake.
  • Using pressure-treated / impregnated (green) lumber anywhere in the sauna, even framing: the preservative chemicals off-gas toxic fumes under heat. Use untreated kiln-dried wood only.
  • Varnishing or lacquering the interior to 'protect' or 'finish' it: the film traps moisture, blisters, and releases harmful fumes when hot. Leave raw or use breathable paraffin/sauna oil only.
  • Choosing dense native hardwoods (coigue, rauli) for the bench top because they look nicer: they hold more heat than alamo and can feel uncomfortably hot to sit on; keep hardwoods for walls/ceiling.
  • Buying knotty pino tinglado without hand-picking: knots bleed sap and get hot; for any board near skin, select the clearest pieces or just use alamo.
  • Exposed metal fasteners on bench surfaces: hot screw/nail heads burn skin — always countersink and plug, or use hidden fixing.
  • Assuming cedar/abachi are easy to get in Chile and budgeting around them — they are scarce and costly here; design around alamo + pino instead.
07

🛋️ Interior

Benches, door, glass, lighting, fixtures

The interior fit-out is where a budget DIY sauna lives or dies on comfort and safety. For a 1.7 x 3.0 m trailer cabin you have room for an L-shaped or single-run two-tier bench: a high tier deep enough to lie down (60 cm) and a low tier to climb on and to rest feet. The two non-negotiable safety rules are: every fastener that could touch skin must be hidden (screw from underneath, or countersink deep and plug), and the door must swing OUTWARD with no lock and only a magnetic or roller-ball catch, so a person who faints can be pulled out. In Chile, classic sauna woods (cedar, Nordic spruce, aspen/abachi) are scarce or imported and expensive, so the practical play is: hidden pine-radiata framing (cheap, everywhere) clad in a low-resin, low-knot wood for the surfaces you actually touch (alamo/poplar is the best-value local match for aspen; alerce/cipres are premium native options). Avoid knotty pino radiata on bench tops and anywhere skin rests because the knots bleed resin and conduct heat. Most fixtures (thermometer/hygrometer, bucket and ladle, IP65 heat-tolerant lighting) are sourceable on MercadoLibre Chile or Sodimac for modest CLP.

Two-tier bench dimensions for a 1.7 x 3.0 m cabin

Upper (foot/sit-and-lie) bench: 89-107 cm off the floor, 51-61 cm deep (use the full 60 cm so an adult can lie flat). Lower bench: 43-48 cm off the floor, 36-51 cm deep (min 40 cm to be usable). Keep the gap between tiers ~46 cm so you can step up easily, and leave at least 109-119 cm of clear headroom above the upper bench. Allow ~60 cm of bench length per seated person and at least 190 cm of clear run if anyone will lie down — your 3.0 m wall easily covers a full-length recline.

[48] How to Build Sauna Benches: Materials, Dimensions, Construction — Haven of Heat ↗

Put the bench high relative to the heater (löyly zone)

The upper bench should sit roughly at or just above the top of the heater stones so your body is in the hot 'sweat zone' where steam from the rocks rises. Plan the bench height first, then choose/position the heater so the rock tops are near upper-bench level; if the bench is far below the stones you sit in a cool layer and the sauna feels weak. A practical target is upper bench around 100-110 cm with the heater stones at a similar height, and ~46 cm of vertical separation to the lower bench/feet.

[49] Sauna Ceiling & Bench Height Masterclass — Haven of Heat ↗

Hidden fasteners: screw from underneath, never from the top

Build a hidden frame from cheap construction lumber (pino radiata 2x4 / 50x100 mm equivalent) with cross-braces every ~41 cm. Then run the finish boards across the top and fasten them ONLY from underneath, up into the frame, using stainless-steel screws (316 stainless is best; it never rusts and stays cool enough to be safe). No screw head should ever be on a surface skin touches. If you absolutely must fasten from above, countersink well below the surface and plug with a wood dowel. Leave a 1 cm gap between surface boards so water drains and air circulates, and a few mm gap to the walls.

[50] A better sauna bench — SaunaTimes ↗

Bench and skin-contact wood: use low-resin, low-knot species

Bench tops, backrests and headrests must be a wood that does NOT bleed resin, does not splinter, and stays cool to the touch. Recommended: aspen/alamo (poplar), alder (aliso), western red cedar, or thermally-modified pine. Explicitly AVOID pine and spruce with knots on skin-contact surfaces: when heated they 'secretan resina (savia)... se derrite, se pega a la piel, provoca quemaduras' (resin melts, sticks to skin, causes burns). So plain knotty pino radiata is fine for the HIDDEN frame and walls but should not be your bench top.

[32] Cómo elegir la madera adecuada para una sauna — Fluidra ↗

Door must open outward, no lock, only a magnetic/ball catch

Outward swing is a safety/egress requirement (echoed by IBC and health codes): if someone faints inside, rescuers must pull the door open even if a body is slumped against it. Use NO keyed lock and no inside latch that can jam — just a simple magnetic catch or roller-ball catch (or a cheap spring closer) that holds the door shut but pops open with a push of body weight. Test it with wet palms. Mount strap or stainless/brass hinges on the outside frame.

[51] How to Build Your Own Sauna Door — SaunaTimes ↗

Door handle and DIY door build

Inside handle must be WOOD, never metal — a metal handle gets dangerously hot. SaunaTimes builds a door over a 18 mm plywood core: staple foil vapor barrier to the inner face, clad both sides in tongue-and-groove (cedar/low-resin wood), and cut the plywood short at the bottom to leave an air-intake slat for combustion/ventilation. A typical rough opening is ~660 mm × 1.96 m giving ~620 mm × 1.93 m of actual opening. You can cut a window and silicone-set a small tempered glass panel (30×30 cm is typical).

[51] How to Build Your Own Sauna Door — SaunaTimes ↗

Glass vs solid door: efficiency tradeoff

Only tempered safety glass may be used — it resists thermal shock and breaks into small rounded pieces, not shards. A solid 40–50 mm wood door loses only ~5-10% of heat through the door area; a full glass door loses ~35-50% (glass R-value is near zero). For a small, occasionally-moved trailer sauna heated to temperature, a solid wood door (or a mostly-wood door with a small tempered window for light and to reduce the closed-in feeling) is the cheaper, more efficient, easier-to-build choice. A full glass door is mainly worth it for looks/claustrophobia and needs good weatherstripping.

[52] Sauna Door Guide: Glass vs. Wood, Sizing & Installation — Peak Primal Wellness ↗

Lighting: mount LOW, use heat/water-rated fixtures

Because heat rises, mount lights LOW — under the bench front, behind a backrest, or low on the wall — not up at the ceiling where it is hottest. Any fixture must be rated for continuous high temperature (look for ~90°C / 194°F rating) and have a high IP rating: IP65 minimum, IP67/IP68 for anything near the bench or exposed to splashed water/löyly. Heat-rated LED strip behind a wooden light shield gives a soft glare-free glow and runs cooler than a hot bulb. Never put an unrated standard bulb high in the cabin — its electronics cook and fail.

[53] Sauna Lighting Guide: Safe and Relaxing Sauna Room — SuperLightingLED ↗

Duckboard floor and removable bench/backrest panels

Build a removable duckboard floor from 1x3" slats laid with ~1 cm gaps over perpendicular sleepers (one each end, one center), fastened with stainless screws, sitting on small rubber feet so the wood stays out of standing water and dries. Make backrests and even bench tops as removable slatted panels (same gap principle) so you can lift them out to dry and clean — important on a trailer that may sit unused between uses. A slight 10-15 degree backward lean is the ergonomic sweet spot for a backrest.

[54] Sauna Benches — SaunaTimes ↗

Fixtures: thermometer/hygrometer, bucket and ladle

A 2-in-1 analog thermometer + hygrometer (one dial showing °C and % relative humidity) is the standard cheap sauna gauge and is sold on MercadoLibre Chile; mount it at upper-bench head height where you actually sit. A wooden bucket (cubeta/balde) with a long-handled ladle (cucharón/cazo), 4-7 L, lets you throw water on the stones for löyly; wood is preferred over metal so the handle does not get hot. TyloHelo has a Chilean storefront (tylohelo.cl) selling genuine sauna buckets, ladles and accessories if you want a known brand rather than a generic import.

[55] Termómetro de sauna 2 en 1 (termómetro + higrómetro) — MercadoLibre Chile ↗

What it looks like

🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Wood substitution is the key Chile decision: classic aspen/abachi/cedar are imported and pricey. Best-value local match for a low-resin, cool-to-touch bench surface is ÁLAMO (poplar) — Fluidra explicitly lists álamo as a top bench wood. Suppliers like Madefan (Santiago) sell álamo and pine; ask your barraca for álamo cepillado sin nudos.
  • Premium native options sold in southern Chile: ALERCE and CIPRÉS (ciprés de las Guaitecas / ciprés common) are naturally rot-resistant and low-resin; some Chilean artisan sauna builders use álamo, ciprés or roble. Alerce is protected/expensive — buy only certified/recovered alerce, and expect a high price.
  • AVOID for bench tops: knotty pino radiata (the default cheap board everywhere in Chile). Its knots bleed resin and conduct heat — fine for the hidden frame and ceiling, not for skin-contact surfaces. Use clear/knot-free álamo or alder on tops, backrests, headrests.
  • Framing and wall cladding: pino radiata machihembrado (tongue-and-groove) is cheap and everywhere — Arauco, Barraca La Estrella, Madefan, Construmart, Sodimac, Easy all carry it kiln-dried (~15% humidity). Use it for the hidden bench frame, ceiling and walls.
  • Tempered glass (vidrio templado): cut-to-size is sold across MercadoLibre Chile and by local vidrierías; a small window panel is cheap, a full tempered glass door is much more. Order a small panel for the door and silicone it in.
