  • Heat/water-rated lighting: IP65 LED fixtures and strips are on MercadoLibre Chile and at Led Studio Chile / Sodimac; confirm the temperature rating, not just IP. Generic IP65 floodlights are cheap but verify they tolerate sauna heat — safest is to mount low.
  • Stainless steel screws (tornillos inox / A2 or A4/316): available at Sodimac, Easy, Construmart and ferreterías. A4/316 is the marine-grade equivalent and the safest for the hot, humid sauna interior.
  • Accessories (termómetro/higrómetro, balde y cucharón) are on MercadoLibre Chile; TyloHelo Chile (tylohelo.cl) is a real in-country sauna-accessory supplier for brand-name bucket/ladle and gauges.
✅ Tips
  • Set the upper bench height FIRST, then position the heater so the rock tops sit near bench level — this is what makes the löyly feel strong.
  • Use clear (knot-free) álamo only on surfaces you touch; save the cheaper knotty pino radiata for the hidden frame and walls. This single substitution is the biggest comfort/safety win on a budget.
  • Pre-drill and screw bench boards from underneath with A4/316 stainless. Buy a cheap stainless screw assortment and a Torx bit so heads don't strip in hard wood.
  • Make the bench tops, backrests and floor duckboard REMOVABLE slatted panels — on a trailer that sits unused, you want to lift everything out to dry and prevent mold.
  • Door: wooden handle inside, magnetic/roller-ball catch only, outward swing. Add a cheap spring closer so it never gets left open and bleeds heat.
  • Leave a bottom air-intake gap at the door (cut the door slightly short) for ventilation and, if you go wood-fired, combustion air.
  • Mount the thermometer/hygrometer at head height by the upper bench, not by the door, so it reads where you actually sit.
  • For lighting, a heat-rated LED strip hidden behind a wooden shield under the upper bench is the safest, cheapest, most pleasant option — no hot bulb at face height.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Using knotty pino radiata on bench tops or backrests — resin oozes when hot, sticks to and burns skin. The single most common DIY mistake in Chile.
  • Exposed metal: top-driven screw heads, a metal door handle, or visible hinge hardware inside reach burning temperatures. Hide all fasteners; use a wood handle.
  • An inward-opening door or any lock/jamming latch — a fainted bather can block an inward door, and a lock can trap someone. Both are genuine safety hazards.
  • A full glass door without good weatherstripping and a small heater — you lose 35-50% of heat through the glass and the sauna struggles to reach temperature.
  • Mounting lights high near the ceiling with a non-heat-rated bulb/fixture — the electronics overheat and fail, and you risk a fixture failure right where it's hottest.
  • Sealing/varnishing interior wood — never varnish or oil sauna interior surfaces; the finish off-gasses and can blister. Leave wood bare (only a sauna-rated paraffin oil on benches if anything).
  • Bench depth too shallow (<50 cm) on the top tier — you can't lie down and the sauna feels cramped despite the 3 m length being available.
  • Duckboard or bench panels screwed down permanently with no airflow underneath — on an occasionally-used trailer this traps moisture and breeds mold and rot.
08

💨 Ventilation

Ventilation & löyly (the steam experience)

Ventilation is the single most under-built part of most DIY saunas, and on a sealed trailer cabin it is doubly important: you need fresh oxygen for breathing (and, with a wood stove, for safe combustion and CO removal), plus a working air loop so the heat reaches your body evenly and the loyly (steam from water on the rocks) feels soft instead of stale and heavy. The classic budget setup is a low fresh-air intake near or under the heater, and an adjustable exhaust on the opposite wall below the upper bench, with a second high vent used only for drying out the cabin after a session. A wood stove largely creates its own draft through the chimney (so a passive low intake near the stove can be enough), while an electric heater has no draft and benefits from a small mechanical exhaust fan to guarantee airflow. Good loyly also depends on the stones: you want a solid mass of dense, porous volcanic rock (basalt/olivina) so the surface stays hot and turns water into fine steam, and in Chile these are cheap and easy to source. Build every vent adjustable so you can tune it through Chile's cold winters and milder shoulder seasons.

Low intake near the heater, adjustable exhaust on the opposite wall below the bench

The standard convection layout puts the fresh-air intake low, near the base of the heater, so incoming air is heated immediately and rises, and the main exhaust diagonally opposite the heater below bench level, around 400 mm off the floor. This pulls warm, fresh air down across the bathers before it exits, instead of dumping your heat and steam straight out. Add a second vent at/near the ceiling that you open only AFTER the session to dry the cabin. Make every vent adjustable (a sliding plate) so you can tune airflow.

[56] Sauna Ventilation: Why It Matters and How to Get It Right - Finnmark Sauna ↗

Wood vs electric: the wood stove drafts itself, the electric heater needs help

A wood-burning stove pulls combustion air and creates draft through its chimney flue, so natural ventilation works fairly well with just a low intake near the stove. An electric heater has no combustion and no draft, so natural convection in an electric sauna is unreliable and rarely moves enough air; a small inline/mechanical exhaust fan is recommended to guarantee fresh air. This matters for your trailer because the choice of heater directly changes how much ventilation hardware you need to build.

[57] Trumpkin's Notes On Building A Sauna - LocalMile ↗

Downdraft ventilation protects the loyly instead of killing it

Trumpkin's widely-cited notes argue for downdraft ventilation: fresh supply high (near the ceiling over the heater) and mechanical exhaust low on the back wall below the foot platform. This works with the convective loop, removes exhaled CO2, keeps feet and legs warm, kills mold/bacteria on the foot bench, and crucially enhances rather than exhausts the steam. An exhaust placed in the ceiling during use sucks your steam and heat straight out and kills the loyly before you feel it. A high vent is for drying AFTER the session, not for use during it.

[58] Proper Ventilation For Electrically Heated Saunas - LocalMile ↗

Vent sizing and air-change targets you can build to

For a family-sized hot room, aim for an intake of at least 160 cm², with 195 cm² better, and avoid restrictive grills that choke airflow. The whole-room target is roughly 6 air changes per hour during use, and the human-air rule of thumb is about 9-12 L/s (20-25 CFM) per person to keep CO2 below ~700 ppm. For your ~1.7 x 3.0 m trailer (a small 2-3 person hot room), a single ~100 mm round adjustable intake and a similar-sized adjustable exhaust, plus one high drying vent, covers it.

[59] Sauna venting: everyone has an opinion - SaunaTimes ↗

Ventilation is what makes steam into loyly, not just hot fog

Loyly is the burst of humid steam when water hits the hot stones; it enters the body through breath and skin. But steam thrown into stale, oxygen-depleted air is just stale air with steam in it, not loyly. Proper fresh-air circulation lets you breathe deeply and feel the oxygenation and thermal effect, and makes the steam feel soft rather than harsh and suffocating. Wood-heated rooms tend to give softer steam than electric. So ventilation is not just safety, it is directly the difference between a good and a bad sauna.

[60] Good sauna vs. bad sauna: one simple way to tell the difference - SaunaTimes ↗

Stones are the thermal engine: use dense porous volcanic rock and enough of it

Sauna stones are the thermal mass that converts dry heat into loyly. Use high-density igneous rock (olivine diabase, peridotite, basalt) 5–10 cm across for an electric heater; these resist cracking and hold heat so the surface stays hot enough to flash water into fine steam. A residential heater typically holds 30-70 kg of stones depending on model. Rinse stones before first use, stack them loosely so air and water pass through, and replace any that crumble. More stone mass = a longer, softer loyly.

[61] HUUM Sauna Heater Stones | Olivine Diabase Granite - Sun Valley Saunas ↗

Wood stove: dedicate a combustion-air intake and fit a CO detector

If you go wood, the stove needs its own fresh-air intake near it so there is enough oxygen for complete combustion; insufficient air causes incomplete burning, smoke and carbon monoxide buildup. CO is colorless and odorless. In a small sealed trailer this is a real safety issue: provide a low outside-air inlet near the stove, keep the chimney/flue clear and properly drafted, burn only seasoned dry hardwood, and install a battery CO detector in the cabin. This combustion-air vent is separate from your comfort ventilation loop.

[38] Ventilation and Drafting a Sauna Stove - KUUMA ↗

Humidity control: open the high vent and dry the cabin after every session

On a wooden trailer cabin, trapped moisture rots timber and grows mold. The drying vent at ceiling level should be opened after use so humid air escapes and the cabin dries fully. During use you control humidity by how much water you throw on the stones and by your adjustable vents; after use you switch to full drying. Leaving the door cracked and the high vent open for an hour post-session is the cheapest mold prevention you can do.

[56] Sauna Ventilation Guide - Finnmark Sauna ↗
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Sauna stones are cheap and local in Chile: Saunatec (saunatec.cl) sells 10 kg of porous selected basalt (piedra volcanica) for about $45.000 CLP. Basalt/olivina from Chile's volcanic geology is exactly the right rock type, so there is no need to import Finnish stones.
  • MercadoLibre Chile (listado.mercadolibre.cl/piedras-volcanicas-para-sauna) lists volcanic sauna stones roughly $3.990 to $47.000 CLP, and many electric calefactores ship bundled with stones (e.g. a 4.5 kW cabin heater + 5 kg volcanic stones, max ~10 kg capacity).
  • Do NOT reuse random river/beach stones or decorative landscaping rock: non-igneous or layered stones can trap water, crack and even explode when heated. Stick to the dense porous volcanic basalt sold specifically for saunas.
  • Bench and interior wood: alamo (poplar) is the budget-friendly Chilean go-to for benches because it does not bleed resin, stays cool to the touch, is soft and splinter-resistant. Native coigue and lenga are also used locally and have good physical properties for sauna/tina work.
  • AVOID resinous pine (pino radiata/insigne is everywhere and cheap but oozes hot resin that burns skin) for benches and any skin-contact surface. If you use pino radiata, restrict it to the structure/exterior, not the bench tops or backrests.
  • All sauna wood should be kiln-dried (secado en horno) to survive the humidity/heat cycling, per Chilean sauna wood guidance.
  • Adjustable vents: cheap circular adjustable vents (rejilla de ventilacion regulable circular) are on MercadoLibre Chile; for the hot interior face, a simple sliding alamo wood cover over a 100 mm duct is the traditional, heat-safe, low-cost solution.
  • Buy a CO detector (detector de monoxido de carbono) at Sodimac/Easy if you choose a wood stove. On a small sealed trailer this is non-negotiable safety hardware, not optional.
✅ Tips
  • Build all vents adjustable. A sliding wooden plate over each duct lets you choke airflow in cold central-southern winters to hold heat, and open up in milder weather or when the cabin feels stuffy.
  • Put the intake low and as close to the heater as practical so cold incoming air is heated instantly and joins the convection loop instead of pooling as a cold draft at floor level.
  • Place the working exhaust on the wall opposite the heater, below the upper bench (around 400 mm off the floor), and a separate small vent near the ceiling used only to dry the cabin after the session.
  • If you go electric, budget a small quiet inline duct fan for the exhaust. Test the noise before final install; a noisy fan ruins the calm of the sauna.
  • Fill the heater fully with stones and stack them loosely, not packed tight, so water and air pass through. Throw water in small ladles onto the hottest part for fine soft loyly rather than one big harsh blast.
  • Throw water only on the stones, never directly on electric elements or the metal body of the heater.
  • Rinse new stones with clean water before first firing to wash off dust, and re-stack/replace crumbling stones every season or two.
  • Crack the door and open the high drying vent for ~30-60 min after each session to dry the timber and prevent mold on the trailer's wooden interior.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Putting the exhaust in the ceiling and running it during the session. It sucks your heat and steam straight out and kills the loyly before you feel it. The ceiling vent is for drying afterward only.
  • Assuming an electric sauna ventilates itself. With no combustion draft, passive airflow is unreliable; without a fan or a well-designed low/high vent pair the room goes stale and stuffy.
  • Sealing the trailer airtight with no fresh-air intake. You will run out of oxygen, build up CO2, and with a wood stove risk dangerous carbon monoxide. Always provide a real fresh-air inlet.
  • Wood stove with no dedicated combustion-air vent and no CO detector. Incomplete combustion from starved air produces odorless, deadly carbon monoxide in a small sealed cabin.
  • Using too few stones or the wrong rock. A near-empty heater or non-volcanic/layered stones give weak, harsh steam and stones can crack or explode. Fill it with proper dense porous basalt/olivina.
  • Skipping the post-session dry-out. Trapped humidity rots the trailer's wooden frame and grows mold fast.
  • Using resinous pino radiata for benches. Hot resin blisters bare skin. Keep skin-contact surfaces to alamo or a non-resinous native wood.
  • Choking airflow with a restrictive decorative grille on the intake. Keep the free area at least ~160–195 cm² so the convection loop actually moves air.
◔

🧰 Materials at a glance

So you recognise it at the barraca — “oh, that!”

Every key material, what it is, and where to find it in the RM. Prices are indicative (2026, CLP).

09

🛒 Where to buy

Local sourcing map for Santiago / RM

For a trailer sauna in central-southern Chile, almost everything except the heater and a few specialty trims can be bought off the shelf at Sodimac, Easy, Construmart, Imperial or a local barraca/maderera. The big-box chains cover framing pine (pino dimensionado seco), tongue-and-groove cladding (machihembrado), glass-wool insulation with aluminum foil facing, high-temp silicone and stainless fasteners. The two things to source carefully are (1) the bench/cladding wood, since classic Nordic spruce/cedar/abachi are scarce and pricey here, so you adapt to álamo (poplar), tilo, or native lenga/coigüe/raulí; and (2) the heater, where you choose between cheap Chinese 220V electric units on MercadoLibre or a Chilean-built wood stove (Nordicasa, Buena Caldera, Tinas Calientes). Dedicated sauna parts (volcanic stones, heaters, glass doors) come from MercadoLibre Chile and a handful of Chilean importers/builders like Saunatec, Comercializadora Saunas (Harvia agent), Tecnic Sauna and Saunas Osorno. Budget the heater as your single biggest line item; the wood and insulation are comparatively cheap.

Framing lumber: pino dimensionado seco at Sodimac/Construmart/Easy

Use kiln-dried pine framing (pino dimensionado SECO, ~12-15% humidity, never 'verde'/green which warps) for the wall and bench studs. At Sodimac, Pino Dimensionado Seco 2x2 3,2 m and 2x3 3,2 m are stock items; the green equivalents run roughly $1.750 CLP (2x2) and $2.680 CLP (2x3) per 3,2 m piece, with dry/cepillado a bit more. Construmart and Easy carry the same 2x2, 2x3 and 2x4 in 3,2 m lengths. For a 1,7 x 3,0 m footprint you need maybe 25-35 pieces. Avoid impregnated/treated pine (arsenic/copper salts) anywhere near the hot interior; reserve impregnado only for the trailer-contact base frame.

[62] Pino Dimensionado Seco 2x3 3,2 m | Sodimac Chile ↗

Cladding and ceiling: pino machihembrado / tinglado (cheapest option)

The default budget interior lining is tongue-and-groove radiata pine: 'Pino Machihembrado Seco Cepillado' (e.g. 3/4 x 4 x 3,2 m, and a thinner 1/2 x 4 'cielo' version for the ceiling) at Sodimac, Construmart (3/4 x 5 x 3,2 m, pino radiata dried to ~12%) and Easy. ARAUCO's MSD line is the brand behind much of it. It is cheap and easy, BUT radiata pine is resinous: knots can weep hot sap. If you go this route, hand-pick low-knot boards, keep pine OFF direct skin-contact bench tops, and accept it for walls/ceiling only.

[63] Pino Seco Machihembrado 19×125 mm × 3,2 m | Construmart ↗

Bench wood: álamo (poplar) is the local sauna-grade choice; native woods as upgrade

For benches and backrests (direct skin contact) the right local pick is álamo/poplar: it secretes no resin, doesn't splinter, stays cool to the touch and is hypoallergenic. Sauna-wood guides explicitly recommend álamo (and tilo/linden for sensitive skin) and warn AGAINST resinous spruce/fir and against abedul/birch for poor durability. Buy álamo cepillado (planed boards) from a barraca/maderera (Madefan, Maderas Llaima, local álamo suppliers) or MercadoLibre 'tabla álamo'. Native upgrades sold by specialty mills (Maderas Tranapuente, Añihue) include raulí cepillado, lenga and coigüe, which are stable and attractive but cost more. Whatever the species, leave a 1-2 cm air gap behind cladding and never finish interior wood with varnish.

[64] Maderas para Saunas: Características y Mejores Tipos ↗

Insulation + vapor barrier in one: foil-faced glass wool at Sodimac

Sodimac stocks 'Lana De Vidrio 50 mm Con Una Cara Con Foil De Aluminio' (50 mm glass wool with an aluminum-foil face) which gives you insulation and the reflective vapor barrier in a single product, exactly what a sauna wall needs. Plain mineral wool 50 mm (AISLAN R122, 0,5x1,2 m) is about $49.000-52.000 CLP per pack and also sold by Easy, Imperial and Socodima. Install the foil facing toward the hot interior, tape all seams with aluminum tape, then batten and clad over an air gap. Glass/mineral wool tolerates sauna heat; do NOT use polystyrene/EPS 'plumavit' or PUR foam on hot walls.

[65] Lana De Vidrio 50 mm Con Una Cara Con Foil De Aluminio | Sodimac Chile ↗

Electric heater (220V): cheap Chinese units on MercadoLibre Chile

If you go electric (220V grid available), the budget path is a 4,5 kW 220V monophase sauna heater (brands like Keya) on MercadoLibre Chile, frequently bundled with 5 kg of volcanic stones and an integrated or separate control. A 4,5 kW unit suits a small ~5-8 m3 room, which matches your trailer footprint. Listings span roughly $59.990 to $438.000 CLP depending on power, brand and whether a digital controller is included; many ship free nationwide and offer cuotas sin interés. Rule of thumb: ~1 kW per m3 of room volume. A 4,5 kW unit on 220V draws ~20 A, so verify your trailer feed and breaker.

[66] Calefactor Electrico Para Sauna | MercadoLibre Chile ↗

Wood stove option: Chilean-made sauna heaters (Nordicasa, Buena Caldera, Tinas Calientes)

For a wood-fired sauna, several Chilean makers build steel stoves to Finnish patterns. Nordicasa (Puerto Varas) sells a 'Estufa Sauna a Leña', Finnish design made in Chile, 60x60x100 cm, for 8-18 m3 rooms, including volcanic stones and chimney, at about $1.290.000 CLP (made-to-order, ~4 weeks). Buena Caldera lists an 'Estufa Leña Sauna 15B'. Tinas Calientes (Puerto Varas) sells interior wood heaters from ~$559.300 CLP, XL ~$583.000, external calderas ~$797.300, and packages from ~$652.000 CLP. A wood stove needs an insulated flue, a 1x1 m non-combustible base and clearances, but no electrical permit.

[30] Estufa Sauna a Leña - Nordicasa ↗

Volcanic sauna stones: Saunatec and MercadoLibre

Heater stones are peridotite-type volcanic rock, prized for being porous and heat-stable. Saunatec Chile sells a 10 kg box of 'Piedras Volcánicas Calefactor Sauna' for about $45.000 CLP; MercadoLibre Chile has many 'piedras volcánicas para sauna' listings with free same-day shipping, and Tinas Calientes sells a stone box too. A small heater needs roughly 5-15 kg. Rinse stones before first use, stack loosely for airflow, and replace any that crack. Do not substitute random river/garden rocks (they can explode when heated).

[67] Piedra Volcánica Sauna 10kg - Saunatec ↗

Glass door and complete kits: Harvia agent + Chilean builders

Tempered-glass sauna doors (typically 8 mm smoky tempered glass, wood or aluminum frame) are sold on MercadoLibre Chile ('puerta vidrio templado sauna') and via importers. Comercializadora Saunas (ventas@harvia.cl, Santiago, +56 9 7156 7500) is the Chilean Harvia representative and supplies heaters, controls, stones, doors and full custom cabins with installation. Tecnic Sauna (40+ years, Finnish saunas) and Saunas Osorno also build and outfit saunas and can supply parts or serve as a price reference. For a budget trailer build, a plain tempered-glass shower/sauna door plus a wood frame you build yourself is the cheapest route.

[68] Venta y Fabricación de Saunas en Chile | Comercializadora Saunas (Harvia) ↗

Sealants and fasteners: high-temp silicone + stainless screws

Around the stove, flue penetration and any glass, use high-temperature silicone (rated ~250-316°C; brands at Sodimac include Agorex, Lanco, Soudal, Ceys; a 300 ml cartridge of 'Silicona para Altas Temperaturas' is a stock item). For everything that touches the hot, humid interior use STAINLESS or hot-dip galvanized screws/nails so heads don't rust and burn skin or stain wood; Sodimac, Easy, Construmart and Imperial all carry stainless terrace/deck screws. Aluminum foil tape for sealing the vapor-barrier seams is sold alongside the lana de vidrio. Avoid plain bright steel fasteners and ordinary acrylic/latex sealants on hot surfaces.

[69] Silicona para altas temperaturas 300 ml | Sodimac Chile ↗

Reference costs

ItemPrice (CLP)Note
Pino dimensionado 2x2 / 2x3 seco (framing), per 3,2 m piece [62]$1.750 - $3.500 CLPGreen prices quoted ($1.750 2x2, $2.680 2x3); dry/cepillado slightly higher. ~25-35 pieces for the frame.
Pino machihembrado/tinglado seco cepillado (wall/ceiling cladding), per 3,2 m board [63]$3.000 - $6.000 CLPRadiata pine T&G, Sodimac/Construmart/Easy; thinner 'cielo' for ceiling. Low-knot only; not for bench tops.
Glass wool / mineral wool 50 mm (foil-faced or plain), per pack/roll [65]$49.000 - $80.000 CLPAISLAN R122 50 mm ~$49k-52k; foil-faced lana de vidrio combines insulation + vapor barrier. Easy/Imperial/Socodima also stock it.
Electric sauna heater 4,5 kW 220V (often with stones/control) [66]$59.990 - $438.000 CLPMercadoLibre Chile, brands like Keya. ~$60k-150k for basic Chinese units; certified Harvia gear far higher. Size ~1 kW/m3.
Wood-burning sauna stove, Chilean-made (Nordicasa / Buena Caldera / Tinas Calientes) [30]$559.300 - $1.290.000 CLPTinas Calientes interior from ~$559.300; Nordicasa 8-18 m3 with stones + chimney ~$1.290.000. Made-to-order, ~4 weeks.
Volcanic (peridotite) sauna stones, 10 kg box [67]$45.000 CLPSaunatec 10 kg ~$45k; MercadoLibre cheaper per kg in bundles; many electric heaters include 5 kg.
High-temperature silicone sealant, 300 ml cartridge [69]$4.000 - $9.000 CLPSodimac stock (Agorex/Lanco/Soudal/Ceys), rated ~250-316°C, for flue/glass/stove joints.
Tempered-glass sauna/shower door [70]$120.000 - $730.000 CLPMercadoLibre Chile tempered-glass shower/sauna doors at the low end; branded Harvia glass door ~730 EUR as a high reference.
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Big-box one-stop shops: Sodimac (Homecenter), Easy, Construmart, plus Imperial and Socodima for insulation; all carry pino dimensionado, machihembrado, lana de vidrio/mineral, silicones and stainless fasteners.
  • Barracas/madereras are cheaper than big-box for solid lumber and the only realistic source of álamo and native species: Madefan, Maderas Llaima, Maderas Tranapuente (raulí/native cepillado), Añihue (maderas nativas).
  • Wood-species reality in Chile: Nordic spruce, western red cedar, abachi and aspen are scarce/expensive imports. Local equivalents: álamo (poplar) and tilo for benches/skin contact; raulí, lenga, coigüe for premium native cladding; radiata pine machihembrado as the cheap wall/ceiling option (low-knot only, never on bench tops).
  • Heater sourcing splits two ways: cheap 220V Chinese electric units on MercadoLibre Chile (Keya etc., $60k-$440k CLP), or Chilean-built wood stoves from Nordicasa, Buena Caldera and Tinas Calientes (Puerto Varas region, ~$560k-$1.290k CLP).
  • Dedicated sauna specialists/importers in Chile: Saunatec (stones, parts), Comercializadora Saunas (official Harvia agent, Santiago), Tecnic Sauna, Saunas Osorno, Patagonia Garden (prefab cabins) — useful for heaters, glass doors, controls and as price references even if you self-build.
  • Volcanic (peridotite) heater stones: Saunatec 10 kg ~$45.000 CLP, plus MercadoLibre and Tinas Calientes. Many electric heaters on MercadoLibre already include 5 kg of stones.
  • MercadoLibre Chile is the best aggregator for heaters, stones and glass doors with free shipping and cuotas sin interés; Yapo is worth checking for second-hand stoves, glass doors and surplus lumber to keep the build cheap.
  • Prices in CLP confirmed mid-2026: foil-faced/plain glass wool 50 mm ~$49k-$52k/pack; pino dimensionado ~$1.750-$2.680 per 3,2 m; high-temp silicone 300 ml stock at Sodimac; electric heater 4,5 kW from ~$60k; Chilean wood stove from ~$559k.
✅ Tips
  • Buy a foil-faced glass wool (lana de vidrio con foil de aluminio) to get insulation + vapor barrier in one product, then aluminum-tape every seam; cheaper and faster than separate foil sheeting.
  • Always specify SECO (kiln-dried) lumber, not VERDE (green), or it will warp and crack as the sauna heats and dries.
  • Hand-select machihembrado boards at the barraca for the fewest knots, and use the planed álamo or a native wood for the bench tops your skin actually touches.
  • Get the heater first and size everything else around it: ~1 kW electric per m3 of room volume; your ~1,7x3,0 m trailer at ~2 m height is roughly 8-10 m3, so a 4,5-6 kW electric or an 8-18 m3 wood stove fits.
  • Check Yapo and MercadoLibre 'usado' for a second-hand stove, glass door or leftover native lumber to slash cost; a used trailer build pairs well with used parts.
  • Leave a 1-2 cm ventilated air gap behind the cladding and use only stainless/galvanized fasteners inside so nothing rusts or burns skin.
  • Call Comercializadora Saunas (Harvia) or Tecnic Sauna for a heater/controller quote even if self-building, to benchmark the cheap MercadoLibre units against certified gear.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Using impregnated/treated pine (pino impregnado, green, with copper/arsenic salts) inside the hot room — it off-gasses; keep it only for the base frame in contact with the trailer.
  • Putting resinous radiata pine where skin touches it: hot knots weep sap and can burn. Pine is fine for walls/ceiling (low-knot), not for bench tops — use álamo/tilo/native there.
  • Installing polystyrene 'plumavit' or PUR foam as wall insulation — it cannot take sauna heat. Use only glass wool / mineral wool (lana de vidrio o lana mineral).
  • Skipping or wrongly orienting the foil vapor barrier: the aluminum face must point toward the hot interior, with all seams taped, or moisture wrecks the wall.
  • Buying the cheapest no-name MercadoLibre electric heater without checking kW vs room volume and 220V amperage/breaker — undersized or unsafe wiring is a real risk; have an SEC-authorized electrician sign off the 220V circuit.
  • Varnishing or oil-finishing interior wood — finishes blister and off-gas at sauna temperatures; leave interior wood bare (paraffin oil only on the door handle/exterior).
  • Using ordinary river or garden stones instead of peridotite/volcanic sauna stones — trapped moisture makes them crack or explode when heated.
  • Note Barraca Paraná in the álamo search is in Uruguay, not Chile — buy álamo from a Chilean barraca (Madefan, Maderas Llaima) or MercadoLibre Chile instead.
10

🧯 Mistakes

Common mistakes, maintenance & winterizing

Most DIY sauna failures trace back to four things: a missing or wrongly-installed vapor barrier, poor in/out ventilation, an incorrectly-sized heater, and using the wrong wood or finish on surfaces that touch skin. On a trailer, two more dominate: overloading or unbalancing the axle (water and a wood stove are heavy), and water/rust attacking the steel chassis in wet Chilean winters. For a 1.7 x 3.0 m mobile sauna in central-southern Chile, the build itself is very doable, but the margins for error are thinner than a backyard build because everything is smaller, moves, and sits outdoors. The good news: the classic Nordic woods you read about (cedar, abachi, aspen) have a cheap, locally-grown equivalent here in alamo (poplar), which is genuinely low-resin and stays cool. Get the vapor barrier, ventilation, heater sizing, stove clearances and wood choice right, then maintain it with light frequent drying and seasonal rust/freeze checks, and it will last many years.

Vapor barrier: foil on the WARM side, sealed continuous, never plain plastic

The aluminum-foil vapor barrier goes on the interior (hot) side of the insulation, directly behind the wood paneling, not on the cold/outside face. Use sauna-grade aluminum foil and overlap seams 5–8 cm sealed with foil tape; seal every wiring, light and vent penetration. Do NOT use ordinary 6-mil polyethylene: it off-gases, softens and reflects no radiant heat at 80-100C. The single most common failure is a 'mostly sealed' barrier where moisture sneaks through unsealed seams and wall-to-floor joints and rots the framing.

[19] How to Insulate a Sauna: Vapor Barrier, R-Values, and Common Mistakes - Haven of Heat ↗

Leave a 2 cm air gap behind the paneling

After the foil, install 1x2 or 1x3 furring strips, then nail the tongue-and-groove over those, leaving a 19-25 mm (3/4 to 1 inch) air gap between foil and wood. This gap reflects radiant heat, lets condensation drain instead of soaking the boards, and gives a nailing surface. Skipping it is a hidden cause of warped, blackened paneling a few years in. Target roughly R-15 walls / R-19+ ceiling for a moderate climate; ceiling R-value should exceed wall R-value.

[19] How to Insulate a Sauna: Vapor Barrier, R-Values, and Common Mistakes - Haven of Heat ↗

Ventilation: low intake near the heater, high exhaust on the OPPOSITE wall

Put the fresh-air intake low (about 30 cm off the floor) next to the heater, and the exhaust high near the ceiling on the opposite wall so air crosses the whole room. Vents on the same wall short-circuit and do nothing. Aim for roughly 6 air changes per hour. Do not seal the room airtight or close all vents to 'trap heat' during use; that kills oxygen and air quality and is the number-one cause of musty mold smell because humidity never dries out. In a tiny trailer cabin the exhaust matters even more for fast dry-down.

[71] Sauna Ventilation Guide: Intake, Exhaust & the 6x Air Exchange Rule - ThermalFinn ↗

Heater sizing: about 1 kW per 1.4 m3, round UP, add for glass and steel

Rule of thumb is roughly 1 kW per ~1,4 m³ of interior volume. A 1.7 x 3.0 m sauna with usable interior near 1.5 x 2.6 m and a 2.0 m ceiling is roughly 7.8 m3, so a 4.5-6 kW heater is the right band; a 4.5 kW 220V unit (20 A) is common in Chile. Undersizing means it never reaches 80-90C, runs constantly and burns out early; oversizing dries the air and wastes money. Add about 1 kW per 1 m2 of glass and 10-20% for under-insulated or hard (steel/glass) surfaces, then round up.

[72] How Many Kilowatts Do You Need for a Sauna Heater? - The Sauna Place ↗

Wood-stove clearances and a non-combustible hearth are non-negotiable

If you go wood-fired, keep 400-600 mm (manufacturer-specific) from combustible walls, or use a cement-board/steel heat shield to reduce that. Stovepipe needs ~450 mm to combustibles, or use double-wall pipe (allows ~150 mm). Set the stove on a non-combustible hearth (concrete/tile/steel) extending at least 300 mm on all sides and 450-500 mm in front of the door for falling embers. Use proper insulated chimney through the roof. These setbacks are tight inside a small trailer, which is the strongest argument for going electric on this build.

[34] Sauna Wood Stove Guide: How to Size and Install One - Peak Primal Wellness ↗

Wood stove = mandatory CO detector and dedicated combustion air

A wood stove in a small sealed cabin can produce carbon monoxide if it cannot draw enough air. Install a fresh-air inlet near the stove sized to match exhaust, never run it with all vents closed, and put a battery CO detector inside the sauna. Insufficient air causes incomplete combustion, smoke and CO buildup. For a tiny 1.7 x 3 m trailer where you sit a meter from the firebox, this is a serious safety line, not optional.

[73] Best Practices for Using a Wood-Burning Sauna Heater Indoors - Haven of Heat ↗

Never varnish, paint or seal the interior; benches need low-resin wood

Do not paint, varnish, stain or polyurethane interior wood: it seals the wood (traps moisture, causes cracking and mold) and off-gases VOCs at 80-90C with people breathing. Avoid boiled linseed oil too (solvents and metal dryers off-gas). The only widely-accepted interior treatment is food-grade paraffin sauna oil in thin coats. For benches and backrests use knot-free, low-resin wood that stays cool; avoid resinous pine/spruce on skin-contact surfaces because sap oozes when hot, gets sticky and can burn skin.

[74] How to Finish the Wood in a Sauna - Haven of Heat ↗

Trailer: know your GVWR and keep weight balanced over the axle

Mobile-sauna failures often come down to weight, not towing skill. Know the trailer GVWR, axle rating and tongue weight before building, and weigh components as you go. Water is the most underestimated load (a small tank shifts 100+ kg instantly and sloshes), and a cast-iron wood stove plus stones is heavy and concentrated. Center heavy items over the axle, keep ~10% of total weight on the tongue, and remember insulation + vapor barrier + air gap + cladding on both faces eats a lot of your 1.7 m width, so plan the interior early.

[75] Chassis Considerations for a Mobile Sauna Build - SaunaTimes ↗

Maintenance: light frequent drying beats occasional heavy scrubbing

After every session wipe benches and backrests with a damp cloth and warm water (with the grain), then leave the door and vents open so residual heat dries the cabin; this prevents mold far better than deep cleans. Use towels on benches to keep sweat out of the wood. For grime use a soft brush; for deeper cleaning a 1:1 white vinegar/water spray (sit 15 min, wipe) or diluted baking soda. Bleach (1:10) only for actual mold spots. A small ceiling exhaust fan dramatically cuts mold risk in a humid outdoor cabin.

[76] 12 Proven Ways to Prevent Mold in a Sauna - Heavenly Heat Saunas ↗

Winterizing: drain all water, blow out lines, dry and cover

For a sauna left outdoors through a cold/wet Chilean winter, the lowest-risk approach is full drain-down each autumn: empty the loyly bucket, any tank, and any plumbing, blow out the lines so nothing freezes and cracks, dry the interior, and fit a quality breathable cover. Water frozen in a tank, pipe or pump can burst it (repairs commonly USD 200-1500). If you have any electric freeze-protection feature it only works while powered on, so do not rely on it for an off-grid winter. Heated year-round operation only makes sense if you sauna often.

[77] How to Winterize Your Sauna and Cold Plunge - Redwood Outdoors ↗

Protect the steel chassis from rust before it ever gets wet

On a second-hand Chilean trailer the chassis is the part that will die first. Treat bare/rusted steel with an anticorrosive primer, then topcoat, or use a zinc-rich cold-galvanizing paint (galvanizado en frio) which sacrifices zinc instead of the steel. After each use and through winter, wash off mud, dry it, and grease bolts and hard-to-reach joints. Store it dry and covered when not in use. This is exactly the regime Chilean trailer and coatings suppliers recommend for our wet winters.

[78] Cuida el chasis de tu remolque: tips para prevenir la corrosion - Trailermax ↗

Hidden long-term failure points: corroded hardware and wood movement

By year 1-2, hidden fasteners, electrical terminals and sensor connections corrode in the humid heat, causing erratic temperature readings, shutoffs or tripped breakers. Repeated heat cycling also makes wood check, warp and open gaps. Mitigate with stainless fasteners, generous ventilation/dry-down, kiln-dried vertical-grain boards, and a periodic (yearly) inspection of fasteners, heater terminals, vapor-barrier seams and wood condition; track trends rather than single readings.

[79] Hidden DIY Sauna & Steam Failure Points: Year 5 Reality Check - InHouse Wellness ↗

Reference costs

ItemPrice (CLP)Note
Electric sauna heater, 4.5 kW, 220V (20 A) [80]$219.000 CLPKeya-type unit on MercadoLibre Chile; right power band for ~8 m3 cabin. 4 kW cabin units also listed.
Alamo (poplar) interior cladding / benches [81]Quote from barracaLow-resin, light, humidity-resistant local wood; sold dry and ready to install by W Maderas, Emilio Pohl, Lifewood, Tranapuente. Contact for per-m2 price; budget alternative to imported aspen/cedar.
Cold-galvanizing zinc paint (galvanizado en frio) for chassis [82]QuoteCorium C-78 zinc-rich paint, sacrificial cathodic protection for the steel trailer frame; from Magna Industrial.
🇨🇱 Chile / Santiago specifics
  • Alamo (poplar) is the standout local sauna wood: grown from Region IV to XI, white, light, low-resin, dimensionally stable and notably resistant to humidity/rot. It is the cheap Chilean stand-in for aspen/abachi and is the right choice for benches and backrests (skin contact). Sold as revestimiento/machihembrado by barracas like W Maderas, Maderas Emilio Pohl, Lifewood and Tranapuente.
  • Avoid pino radiata (the cheapest board at Sodimac/Easy/barracas) on benches and any skin-contact surface: it has knots and resin pockets (bolsas de resina) that ooze sticky sap and can burn skin when hot. It is acceptable only for hidden framing, never for the hot interior face if you can use alamo instead.
  • For tinas/saunas, Chilean builders traditionally use alamo, cipres and roble for their natural rot/fungal resistance; alamo tubs are quoted at roughly 10-12 years of life, which tells you alamo handles humidity well.
  • Native woods coigue, rauli and lenga are sold by barracas (maderanativachile.cl, Maderera Nacional, Tranapuente) but are denser/pricier and not necessary for a budget build; coigue is light and workable if you want a feature bench but alamo is cheaper.
  • Electric heaters are readily available locally on MercadoLibre Chile at 220V: a 4.5 kW unit (20 A, monophase 220V or 380V) around CLP $219.000, and 4 kW cabin heaters also listed. With grid power present and the tight clearances of a 1.7 m cabin, electric is the simpler, safer pick over a wood stove for this trailer.
  • Rust protection products are easy to source here: anticorrosive primers/paints (Cintac, Pinturas Iverzur), and cold-galvanizing zinc paint such as Corium C-78 from Magna Industrial. Wash road salt/mud off and re-grease bolts each winter.
  • Aluminum foil vapor barrier and foil tape, plus rock-wool/glass-wool insulation, are stocked at Sodimac, Easy and Construmart; do not substitute the foil with the cheap polyethylene rolls sold for under-slab use.
✅ Tips
  • Given 220V is available and clearances are tight in a 1.7 m cabin, default to a 4.5 kW electric heater; reserve the wood-stove option only if you accept the hearth/chimney/CO complexity.
  • Build benches and all skin-contact surfaces in alamo (poplar); keep cheaper pino radiata only for hidden framing.
  • Mount a removable bench so you can lift it out to dry and scrub the floor underneath, where moisture and mold collect.
  • Install a small 12V or 220V ceiling exhaust fan to force dry-down after each session; the cabin is small so even a tiny fan clears humidity fast.
  • Treat and seal the trailer chassis (anticorrosive primer or cold-galvanize) BEFORE you build the cabin on top, while you still have full access.
  • Slope the floor slightly to a drain and use a non-slip, non-combustible floor (tile or treated concrete board) under and around the heater.
  • Keep a written weight tally as you build and confirm you are under the trailer GVWR and axle rating before the first tow.
  • Store a battery CO detector and a small fire extinguisher inside; if wood-fired the CO detector is mandatory.
  • Each autumn: drain the loyly bucket and any water/plumbing, blow out lines, dry the interior, and fit a breathable cover.
⛔ Mistakes to avoid
  • Putting the vapor barrier on the cold/outside face, or using ordinary polyethylene instead of aluminum foil; it off-gases, doesn't reflect heat, and rots the framing.
  • Leaving seams, pipe and wire penetrations unsealed so the 'mostly sealed' barrier leaks moisture into the walls.
  • Omitting the air gap behind the cladding, leading to warped, blackened boards.
  • Both vents on the same wall, or sealing the cabin airtight / closing vents during use, which causes musty mold smell and (with a stove) CO risk.
  • Undersizing the heater so it never reaches temperature and burns out, or oversizing so it bakes and dries the air.
  • Varnishing, painting or polyurethane-sealing the interior wood, or using boiled linseed oil; off-gasses and traps moisture.
  • Using resinous knotty pino radiata on benches where hot sap oozes and burns skin.
  • Wood stove with inadequate clearances, no non-combustible hearth, no proper insulated chimney, or no CO detector.
  • Overloading the trailer or putting heavy water tank/stove off-axis so it tows unsafely and sloshes.
  • Skipping chassis rust treatment, then letting mud and winter wet sit on bare steel.
  • Leaving water in a bucket, tank or pipe over a freezing winter, cracking the component.
  • Heavy occasional scrubbing instead of wiping and drying after every session, which lets mold establish.
  • Ignoring hidden corroded fasteners and heater terminals until they cause shutoffs or tripped breakers.
∑

🧮 Budget calculator

Pick size, quality and materials — get a live CLP estimate

Set your cabin dimensions, choose a build quality and swap individual materials. The estimate updates live, sizes the heater to the volume, and checks the built weight against typical single-axle trailer ratings. Figures are indicative ranges (Chile, 2026), not a quote.

0750 kg axle1500 kg axle

Itemised estimate

ItemCLP (low–high)
Estimated total —

Built weight is the empty, ready-to-tow estimate (structure + heater + stones + benches + door), excluding people and water. Recommended heater power uses ≈1 kW per m³ of usable volume. Swap any material above to see the effect. All rates derived from the sourced research on this page.

$

💰 Budget breakdown

Two reference tiers from the research

Assumptions
  • Trailer is already owned at ~500.000 CLP; it is included in both grand totals as the user requested.
  • Trailer is a small carro de arrastre in the 500-750 kg cargo class (no freno), already with patente/papeles al dia. SOAP (~3.900/yr) and permiso de circulacion (~15.000-30.000/yr) are recurring and NOT included in the build total; revision tecnica (~5.000-10.000) is a one-off shown only as a note.
  • Interior hot room is roughly 1.6 m x 2.2 m x 2.0 m high = ~7 m3 air volume, ~3.5 m2 floor. Wall + ceiling cladding area ~18-22 m2, insulation area ~20 m2.
  • Grid 220V is available but the standard 40 A monophase empalme leaves no room for a 6 kW+ electric heater alongside the house, so the LEAN tier uses a small 4.5 kW electric heater (20 A) and the COMFORTABLE tier uses a purpose-built Chilean wood sauna stove (no empalme upgrade, no SEC trifasico cost).
  • Owner is a moderately handy DIYer doing all labor; no paid carpentry/welding labor is budgeted. A SEC-authorized electrician is budgeted in the LEAN tier only for the heater circuit (the one task you cannot legally self-do).
  • Prices are mid-2026 CLP from the gathered Sodimac/Easy/Construmart/MercadoLibre/barraca/maker data. Ranges reflect low-knot vs better stock and Santiago availability.
  • Bench tops and all skin-contact surfaces use alamo (poplar); pino radiata seco (kiln-dried, never impregnated) is used only for hidden framing; pino impregnado only for the exterior sub-floor.
  • Wood stove tier includes the matched factory chimney kit, volcanboard heat shield, hearth and a battery CO detector as non-negotiable safety items.

LEAN build (cheapest viable, electric 4.5 kW, alamo benches over radiata frame, salvaged/basic skin)

$1.085.000 - $1.460.000 CLP
ItemCLPNote
Second-hand trailer (already owned)$500.000Carro de arrastre ~500-750 kg class; included in total per request. Add ~$25.000-45.000/yr for SOAP + permiso de circulacion (recurring, not in build total).
Chassis rust protection (anticorrosive primer + cold-galv zinc paint)$15.000 - $30.000Iverzur/Cintac primer or Corium C-78 zinc paint on the steel frame before building.
Exterior sub-floor / platform: pino impregnado deck + OSB$25.000 - $45.0003-4 planchas OSB 11mm (~$5.500-9.000 ea) or impregnated deck boards. Outdoor sub-floor only, never interior.
Framing: pino dimensionado seco 2x2 / 2x3, ~28 pieces$50.000 - $85.000~$1.750-3.000 per 3,2 m piece. Kiln-dried, untreated, for hidden tabique only.
Exterior skin: pino tinglado impregnado / salvaged siding$45.000 - $80.000~$3.090-4.061 per board; check Yapo for surplus lumber to cut this.
Insulation: glass wool 50 mm (lana de vidrio), ~20 m2$40.000 - $65.000TOPEX/AISLANGLASS rolls; buy unfaced 'libre' so the foil is the only vapor barrier.
Vapor barrier: aluminium foil barrier + foil tape$25.000 - $45.000FSK/foil-faced barrier (Sodimac/Aislacel) + genuine cinta de aluminio (~$8.000-12.000/roll). NOT cloth duct tape, NOT polyethylene.
Interior wall/ceiling cladding: pino tinglado seco cepillado (low-knot)$60.000 - $95.000~$3.090/pieza x ~20-25 boards. Low-knot only; walls/ceiling away from skin contact.
Bench tops + backrests: alamo seco (poplar) ~3-4 m2$60.000 - $110.000Quote from barraca/ML 'alamo seco'. Non-negotiable that skin-contact surfaces are NOT radiata pine.
Electric sauna heater 4.5 kW 220V (Keya-type, often incl. ~5 kg stones)$150.000 - $230.000MercadoLibre; ~$219.000 typical. 20 A draw fits a 220V monophase circuit with headroom.
Extra volcanic stones to fill the box (10-25 kg)$16.000 - $45.000If heater ships with only 5 kg; saco 25 kg ~$16.000 (BuenaCaldera) up to Saunatec 10 kg ~$45.000.
SEC electrician: dedicated heater circuit (breaker + RCD + cable)$60.000 - $120.00020 A circuit, RCD and AWG cable for a 4.5 kW unit. The one task you cannot legally self-do in Chile.
Door: cheap solid/glazed panel siliconed in (no full tempered door)$30.000 - $80.000Small tempered window panel from a vidrieria + DIY alamo frame, or a salvaged door reworked.
Ventilation: 100 mm duct + sliding alamo cover + cheap intake vent$8.000 - $18.000Rejilla regulable on ML for intake; traditional wood sliding cover on the hot face.
Fasteners + high-temp silicone + sealant (stainless inox A2/A4)$25.000 - $45.000Tornillos inox, tirafondos, nyloc, Sika PU under bottom plate, high-temp silicone $4.000-9.000.
IP65 LED light (mounted low) + basic termometro/higrometro + balde/cucharon$25.000 - $55.000Generic IP65 LED on ML; verify heat tolerance, mount low. Cheap gauge + bucket set.
Heater clearances / minimal heat shield (volcanboard offcut)$10.000 - $25.000Volcanboard sheet for behind/under the electric heater; lighter need than a wood stove.

COMFORTABLE build (Chilean wood sauna stove + matched flue, alamo benches, native/alamo cladding, glass door, mineral-wool insulation)

$2.330.000 - $3.520.000 CLP
ItemCLPNote
Second-hand trailer (already owned)$500.000Included in total per request. Recurring SOAP + permiso (~$25.000-45.000/yr) and one-off revision tecnica (~$5.000-10.000) not in build total.
Chassis rust protection (anticorrosive primer + Corium C-78 cold-galv zinc)$25.000 - $45.000Full prep + two coats; re-grease bolts and wash road salt each winter.
Exterior sub-floor: pino impregnado deck + OSB estructural$35.000 - $60.000Arauco DECK / impregnated boards over OSB 11 mm; outdoor platform only.
Framing: pino dimensionado SECO 2x3 / 2x6, ~30 pieces$70.000 - $120.000Kiln-dried certificado (~$7.990 for 2x6 at Easy); choose madera seca so the tabique doesn't twist.
Exterior skin: quality tinglado / fibrocemento or PVC siding$90.000 - $180.000ChileSiding / fibrocemento for a weatherproof road-durable shell.
Insulation: lana mineral (Rockwool-type) 50 mm, ~20 m2$80.000 - $130.000AISLAN lana mineral R122 ~$38.700-49.000/pack; better fire resistance and heat tolerance than glass wool around a stove.
Vapor barrier: FSK aluminium foil barrier + quality foil tape$35.000 - $60.000Aislacel FSK radiant+vapor roll + genuine cinta de aluminio, all seams sealed.
Interior wall/ceiling cladding: alamo / coigue revestimiento, ~18-20 m2$300.000 - $700.000Coigue ~$41.412/m2 or alamo machihembrado (W Maderas/Madefan). Lower end if part pino tinglado on ceiling, upper if all native/alamo.
Bench tops + backrests + headrests: clear alamo seco, ~4 m2$120.000 - $220.000Knot-free alamo cepillado from barraca; finished only with parafina/aceite para sauna, never barniz.
Wood sauna stove (BuenaCaldera 15-series, Chilean-made)$504.000 - $660.000Purpose-built with stone box; confirm m3 rating by WhatsApp. (Nordicasa premium ~$1.290.000 is an upgrade option.)
Matched factory chimney / flue kit (double-wall, roof penetration, cap)$386.000 - $435.000BuenaCaldera kit / full set. Safest path; the roof penetration is the highest fire/leak risk joint to improvise.
Volcanic sauna stones 20-30 kg (basalt/peridotite)$32.000 - $90.000BuenaCaldera saco 25 kg ~$16.000 x2, or Saunatec 10 kg ~$45.000. Dense porous volcanic only, never river rock.
Heat shield + hearth: volcanboard (Clase A1) + zincalum sheet on ceramic spacers$50.000 - $95.000Volcanboard 6-8 mm (~$12.990-19.990/plancha) + plancha acero; protects walls/floor around the stove.
Tempered-glass door (puerta de vidrio templado)$160.000 - $400.000ML tempered shower/sauna door at low end; vidrieria cut-to-size with frame. Branded (Harvia ~730 EUR) far higher.
Battery CO detector (non-negotiable with a wood stove)$10.000 - $30.000AIZO/VIPA autonomous battery unit; works engine-off, off-grid.
Ventilation: intake vent + 100 mm duct + sliding alamo cover$12.000 - $30.000Adjustable rejilla low intake + traditional wood sliding cover near the stove.
Fasteners (stainless A4/316) + high-temp silicone + Sika PU sealant$40.000 - $75.000Marine-grade A4/316 inox for the hot humid interior; high-temp silicone for flue/glass/stove joints.
IP65 heat-rated LED lighting + termometro/higrometro + balde/cucharon set$40.000 - $90.000TyloHelo Chile gauge + bucket/ladle; heat-rated IP65 fixture mounted low.
💡 How to save money
  • Trawl Yapo and Facebook Marketplace for surplus lumber, a used glass/shower door, and even a second-hand sauna stove before buying new; these three lines are where the biggest savings hide.
  • Buy alamo only for the surfaces that touch skin (bench tops, backrests, headrests) and use cheap low-knot pino tinglado for the ceiling and upper walls. This single split can cut the cladding line by half versus all-native.
  • Never substitute the foil vapor barrier with the cheap polyethylene rolls sold for under-slab use, and never use plumavit/aislapol inside the envelope (it fails below sauna temps). Doing it wrong means rot and a rebuild, which is the most expensive 'saving' of all.
  • Use kiln-dried (seco) untreated pino radiata for hidden framing and reserve impregnated pine strictly for the outdoor sub-floor; buying the wrong grade for the wrong place wastes money and risks resin burns or warping.
  • For the LEAN tier, electric avoids the ~$386.000+ chimney kit, the volcanboard hearth and the CO detector entirely; the wood-stove tier's stove + flue + shielding + glass door is roughly $1.1-1.6M of the difference between the two builds.
  • Keep the electric heater at 4.5 kW / 20 A so you stay inside the standard 40 A monophase empalme and avoid a SEC power-increase and 380V trifasico install, which would add hundreds of thousands of CLP.
  • Source volcanic stones by the 25 kg saco from BuenaCaldera (~$16.000) rather than the boutique 10 kg boxes (~$45.000); both are the right basalt, the per-kg price is very different.
  • Buy fasteners, silicone and foil tape in single bulk runs to Sodimac/Construmart rather than piecemeal; and ask the barraca to cepillar (plane) the alamo for you to skip buying/learning a planer.
  • Confirm the trailer already has patente and papeles al dia before building anything on it; homologating a pure 'hechizo' trailer later can cost real time and money via REMUCAR/Registro Civil.
  • Do all framing, cladding and insulation yourself and pay a SEC electrician only for the heater circuit (LEAN) — labor is the largest invisible cost and the one a handy DIYer can fully absorb.
¶
